proposals.title: Adaptive Computational Synthesis: A Retrosynthetic-Informed Framework for Dynamic Quantum Chemical Pathway Planning in Anion Binding Studies
proposals.motivation: Computational studies of anion binding, especially for complex macrocycles or multi-anion complexes, require a careful trade-off between accuracy and cost. Current workflows often apply a single, fixed hierarchy of methods (e.g., DFT → CCSD(T)) to all systems, which is inefficient. An adaptive, molecule-specific strategy is needed to optimally allocate computational resources across diverse molecular targets, from simple halides to large, flexible receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: A computational framework that dynamically plans a tailored sequence of quantum chemical calculations, based on a retrosynthetic analysis of a target molecule's complexity, will predict anion binding affinities and electronic properties with comparable accuracy to high-level benchmarks but at significantly reduced computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a rule-based and machine learning-informed pathway planner. It will first decompose a target anion-receptor complex into structural and electronic 'synthons' (e.g., binding motif rigidity, anion charge density, conformational flexibility). Using a graph representation and a cost-accuracy database of pre-2026 methods (DFT functionals, DLPNO-CCSD(T), neural network potentials like ANI), the planner will construct an optimal calculation sequence. This may involve, for example, using neural network potentials for conformational sampling, specific DFT for geometry optimization, and targeted high-level single-point corrections only where necessary.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source software tool for adaptive computational chemistry, demonstrating its utility by generating a comprehensive and computationally efficient dataset of binding energies for diverse anion-macrocycle complexes. It will establish a new paradigm for 'computational synthesis' that moves beyond one-size-fits-all protocols.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of organic retrosynthetic analysis to computational method selection, creating a dynamic, conditional workflow rather than a static pipeline. It uniquely integrates heuristic chemical insight with data-driven cost-accuracy models to make real-time planning decisions.

proposals.title: A Predictive Map for Perturbative and Quadratic CI Methods: Data-Driven Selection for Anion Binding, Catalysis, and Excited States
proposals.motivation: Selecting an electronic structure method for a new chemical problem is often based on heuristics or computational cost, not predictive accuracy. This is especially critical for systems like anion-receptor complexes, transition metal catalysts, and excitonic dimers, where subtle correlation effects dictate properties. A systematic, quantitative map defining where mid-level methods like MP4 and QCISD succeed or fail is urgently needed to guide efficient and reliable computational studies in these areas.
proposals.hypothesis: The utility of MP4 and QCISD methods can be quantitatively mapped onto a chemical domain space defined by system composition, property type, and desired accuracy, enabling the creation of a predictive protocol for method selection.
proposals.method: We will execute a stratified benchmark of 300+ systems, including organic/inorganic anions, transition metal complexes (e.g., Mo-diimine catalysts), and supramolecular assemblies (e.g., bambusuril hosts). Key properties—anion binding energies, reaction barriers, non-covalent interaction energies, and optical excitation energies—will be computed with MP4(SDQ), QCISD, and QCISD(T), using CCSD(T)/CBS and experimental data as benchmarks. Statistical learning (e.g., decision trees or logistic regression on descriptors like multireference character, orbital gaps, and system size) will be used to derive predictive rules for method performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly accessible, data-driven protocol or software tool that recommends the minimum computationally feasible method to achieve target accuracy for a given chemical problem. It will provide concrete guidance for the computational study of anions, catalysis, and photophysics, directly supporting the PI's research themes while serving the broader community.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond isolated benchmark studies, this project creates a general, predictive framework for method selection. It explicitly links method failure modes to chemically intuitive descriptors, offering actionable guidance rather than retrospective analysis.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the Sufficiency of MP4 and Quadratic CI for Electron Correlation in Anion-Macrocycle Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of non-covalent interactions, especially anion binding by synthetic receptors, is crucial for designing new sensors and catalysts. High-level coupled-cluster (CC) methods are the gold standard but are computationally prohibitive for large macrocycles. Efficient, systematically improvable methods like MP4 and QCI are needed for predictive design, but their sufficiency for these complex, diffuse, and potentially multi-reference anion-binding landscapes is not established.
proposals.hypothesis: For the ground-state electronic structures of anion-macrocycle complexes, the MP4(SDQ) and QCISD(T) methods will provide interaction energies and geometries that are statistically indistinguishable from higher-level CCSD(T) benchmarks across a diverse set of anions and receptor topologies.
proposals.method: We will perform a rigorous benchmark on a dataset derived from Raghavachari's prior computational studies of anion-macrocycle complexes. For each system, we will compute binding energies and optimized geometries using MP4(SDQ), QCISD(T), and CCSD(T) as the reference, employing correlation-consistent basis sets (e.g., aug-cc-pVDZ, aug-cc-pVTZ) with appropriate diffuse functions. Statistical analysis (mean absolute deviations, correlation plots) will quantify the performance. Challenging cases with potential multi-reference character will be identified via T1 diagnostics and further tested with CASSCF/NEVPT2.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, cost-effective computational protocol for the accurate study of anion-receptor interactions, directly impacting the design of new optical anion sensors and electrocatalysts. It will establish clear boundaries for when more expensive coupled-cluster calculations are necessary, streamlining computational workflows in supramolecular chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While MP4 and QCI have been benchmarked for small molecules and main-group thermochemistry, their rigorous assessment for the specific challenges of anion binding—characterized by charge-dipole interactions, dispersion, and polarization in large, flexible hosts—is novel and addresses a direct need in the computational supramolecular community.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic-Driven Composite Protocol for Accurate and Reliable Transition State Energetics in Anion Binding and Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of reaction barrier heights is critical for understanding and designing anion-binding catalysts and electrocatalytic cycles, such as CO2 reduction. However, transition states (TSs) often exhibit challenging electronic structures, including multi-reference character and flat potential energy surfaces, which compromise the reliability of standard single-reference composite methods like CBS-QB3. This leads to significant uncertainties in computational mechanistic studies, hindering predictive catalyst design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite methodology, which first diagnoses the electronic character of a TS and then dynamically selects a protocol optimized for either single- or multi-reference regimes, will yield more accurate and systematically improvable barrier heights with quantified uncertainty.
proposals.method: The method begins with an automated diagnostic step using low-cost calculations (e.g., T1 diagnostics, %TAE) to assess multi-reference character. For single-reference TSs, a new composite protocol will be developed, building on established methods (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS) but with re-parameterized basis set extrapolations and correlation treatments specifically for the diffuse electron densities and partial bonds at TS geometries. For systems with strong multi-reference character, a specialized composite protocol will integrate multi-reference perturbation theory (e.g., CASPT2) with extrapolations to the complete basis set limit. Both pathways will incorporate rigorous error analysis to produce a final barrier height with a confidence interval.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, automated computational tool for TS energetics, directly applicable to the study of anion-binding macrocycles and transition metal catalysts. It will provide the community with a benchmarked protocol that increases reliability and quantifies uncertainty, enabling more confident predictions in mechanistic studies and catalyst screening.
proposals.novelty: The integration of an upfront diagnostic to dynamically route calculations between distinct, TS-optimized composite protocols is a novel, system-aware approach that moves beyond applying a single, static high-level method to all transition states.

proposals.title: High-Accuracy Composite Thermochemistry for Transition States: Extending Gaussian-1-Type Procedures to Reaction Barriers
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of reaction barriers is crucial for modeling catalysis and reaction mechanisms, yet standard density functional theory (DFT) methods often yield unreliable barrier heights. Composite quantum chemical methods like G4 and CBS-QB3 provide excellent thermochemical accuracy for stable molecules but are not systematically parameterized or validated for transition-state structures, limiting their predictive power for kinetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite procedure, analogous to Gaussian-1 (G1) theory but specifically optimized for transition-state geometries, can systematically calculate reaction barrier heights with chemical accuracy (within ~1 kcal/mol) of benchmark data. This will require careful re-parameterization against a high-level wavefunction-based database of transition-state energies.
proposals.method: We will develop a new composite method by adapting the G4(MP2) framework. A training set of ~50 diverse transition-state energies will be constructed using highly accurate CCSD(T)/CBS calculations as benchmarks. The composite scheme will combine a core energy from a moderate-level method (e.g., B3LYP/6-31G(2df,p)) with MP2 and higher-level correction terms, but the empirical parameters (scaling factors, additive corrections) will be optimized via linear regression to reproduce the CCSD(T)/CBS barrier heights for the training set. The method's performance will be validated on an independent test set, including catalytic transition states relevant to CO2 reduction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first composite thermochemical method explicitly designed and validated for transition states, providing a practical tool for predicting kinetics with benchmark accuracy. It will directly enhance computational studies in catalysis and anion binding, where reliable barrier predictions are essential for understanding selectivity and designing new receptors or catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While composite methods are standard for stable species, their systematic extension and re-parameterization specifically for the electronic structure of transition states is a novel and non-trivial advancement, moving beyond the common practice of applying molecule-optimized protocols to transition structures without validation.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Gaussian-1 Theory for Accurate Thermochemistry of Anion-Binding Macrocycles and Transition Metal Complexes
proposals.motivation: The Gaussian-1 (G1) theory, introduced by Pople and co-workers, demonstrated high accuracy for atomization energies of small molecules, but its performance on larger, chemically relevant systems like anion receptors and catalytic intermediates remains untested. Given Dr. Raghavachari's expertise in computational anion binding and electrocatalysis, a rigorous validation on these complex functional molecules is critical to establish G1 as a reliable tool for predictive design in supramolecular and inorganic chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Gaussian-1 theory will maintain its sub-1 kcal/mol accuracy for thermochemical properties (e.g., binding enthalpies, reaction energies) of larger, synthetically relevant anion-binding macrocycles and mononuclear transition metal complexes, despite the increased size and electron correlation challenges.
proposals.method: We will select a benchmark set of 20-25 molecules, including larger bambusuril analogs, diimine-Mo complexes, and other anion receptors from recent literature. G1 energies (using Gaussian 92 or equivalent) will be computed for all species. Benchmark values will be established using higher-level composite methods (e.g., G2 theory where feasible) and experimental data from thermochemical cycles or known binding constants. Statistical analysis (mean absolute deviation, maximum error) will compare G1 performance on this set to its original small-molecule benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first performance benchmark of G1 theory for biologically and catalytically relevant medium-sized molecules, directly informing its utility in computational supramolecular and inorganic chemistry. It will yield a curated dataset of high-accuracy reference energies for important anion-binding and catalytic structures.
proposals.novelty: While G1 was benchmarked on small main-group species, its systematic evaluation on larger, charge-dense systems like macrocyclic anion receptors and open-shell transition metal hydrides—key to this group's research—is novel and addresses a specific gap in method validation.

proposals.title: A Calibrated Reference Network for Systematic Error Correction in Atomic Total Energy Calculations
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of atomic total energies is foundational for computational chemistry and materials science, yet standard methods like density functional theory (DFT) and even coupled-cluster calculations exhibit systematic, element-dependent residual errors. These errors propagate into molecular binding energies, reaction barriers, and spectroscopic predictions, limiting predictive reliability. A robust, generalizable framework for quantifying and correcting these systematic biases is currently lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the systematic errors of standard electronic structure methods for neutral atoms can be modeled as a continuous, predictable function of fundamental atomic properties, and that a network of high-accuracy reference atoms can be used to calibrate and correct these errors across the periodic table.
proposals.method: We will first establish a reference network using atoms (H to Rn) whose total energies are known with benchmark accuracy from high-level quantum methods (e.g., FCI, CCSD(T) with large basis sets). For each target method (e.g., common DFT functionals), we will compute the deviation from the reference energy for all network atoms. Using statistical learning (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression), we will model the error as a function of atomic descriptors (Z, valence electron count, ionization potential, electron affinity). The resulting error function will be validated and then applied as a post-hoc correction to atomic energies outside the training set.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a universally applicable, traceable calibration protocol for atomic energy calculations, significantly improving the accuracy of DFT and other methods for any neutral atom. It will provide the community with open-source correction functions and a quantitative framework for assessing systematic error, enhancing the reliability of downstream molecular and materials simulations.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting a metrological network concept from geodesy to computational quantum chemistry, moving beyond atom-specific benchmarks to a continuous, property-based error model. It systematically addresses the stoichiometry-independent, atomic-scale biases that underlie errors in molecular anion binding and complexation energies, a core theme in the researcher's prior work.

proposals.title: Correcting Systematic Atomic Energy Errors in Density Functional Theory via Anion-Binding-Inspired Pseudopotential Refinement
proposals.motivation: Accurate total energies for neutral atoms are the fundamental building blocks for reliable quantum chemical calculations of molecules and materials. Despite advances, widely used density functionals and pseudopotentials exhibit systematic, element-dependent residual errors in atomic energies, which propagate and compromise the accuracy of calculated binding energies, reaction energies, and redox potentials. Correcting these foundational errors is critical for predictive computational design in catalysis and materials science, areas central to the researcher's prior work on CO2 reduction and anion receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic errors in the total energies of neutral atoms calculated with standard pseudopotentials and density functionals can be significantly reduced by developing a new generation of element-specific, correlation-consistent pseudopotentials that are optimized against highly accurate atomic reference data, inspired by the precision required in modeling anion-receptor binding.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput computational workflow to quantify residual errors for elements across the periodic table using the CCSD(T)/CBS level of theory as the reference standard. For each element, we will refine the parameterization of correlation-consistent effective core potentials (ccECPs) by minimizing the error in the neutral atom's total energy while preserving the accuracy of key atomic properties (e.g., ionization potentials, electron affinities) and molecular test sets. The optimization will utilize a Bayesian framework to efficiently navigate parameter space and ensure transferability, leveraging the researcher's expertise in benchmarking anion binding energetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available set of pseudopotentials that provide near-CCSD(T) accuracy for atomic energies at DFT cost, directly improving the accuracy of calculated formation and binding energies. It will establish a robust, generalizable protocol for pseudopotential refinement, bridging high-accuracy wavefunction methods and practical DFT simulations for complex systems like the electrocatalytic complexes and macrocyclic receptors studied by the group.
proposals.novelty: While pseudopotential development often focuses on valence properties, this work uniquely targets the direct, systematic correction of total energy errors in neutral atoms as a foundational step. It integrates the precision-driven approach from anion-binding benchmark studies with pseudopotential design, a novel synergy for improving DFT's quantitative reliability.

proposals.title: A Multi-Fidelity Computational Framework for Accurate and Efficient Prediction of Anion-Excited State Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurately calculating excited-state energies for anion-macrocycle complexes is critical for designing optical anion sensors and understanding photophysical processes in supramolecular chemistry. However, high-accuracy wavefunction methods like EOM-CCSD are prohibitively expensive for these large systems, while low-cost TD-DFT methods suffer from well-documented errors for charge-transfer and Rydberg states. A systematic, cost-effective strategy to bridge this accuracy-efficiency gap is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, multi-fidelity computational scheme, inspired by structural engineering workflows, can be developed to predict excited-state energies of anion-receptor complexes with near-wavefunction accuracy at a fraction of the cost by strategically guiding high-level calculations with low-fidelity screening.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based workflow that first uses rapid TD-DFT calculations with standard functionals (e.g., ωB97X-D) to screen the excited-state landscape of anion-macrocycle complexes, identifying states with diagnostic features like high charge-transfer character or significant double-excitation contribution via analysis tools (e.g., natural transition orbitals, density difference plots). This low-fidelity map will then direct targeted, high-fidelity single-point energy calculations using selectively applied, expensive methods: EOM-CCSD for localized valence/Rydberg states, and spin-component-scaled CC2 or ADC(2) for larger systems with charge-transfer character. The final composite energy will be assembled via an error-correction protocol trained on a benchmark set of smaller anion-organic complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a general, automated computational protocol for excited-state prediction in supramolecular anion chemistry, directly applicable to the design of next-generation optical receptors. It will provide new physical insights into the photophysics of anion complexes by enabling systematic studies of structure-excited-state relationships at high accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal adaptation of multi-fidelity engineering principles to computational photochemistry, creating a context-aware, decision-based composite method rather than a simple linear correction scheme, specifically tailored for the challenging electronic structure of anion-bound excited states.

proposals.title: Accelerating the Computational Screening of Anion Receptors: A DFT-Based Protocol for Initial Geometry Sampling and Binding Affinity Ranking
proposals.motivation: Computational screening of macrocyclic anion receptors, as in your recent work, relies on accurate but expensive quantum chemical methods for final binding energy calculations. The initial geometry sampling and pre-optimization steps often use lower-level Hartree-Fock (HF) methods, which are a significant bottleneck for large-scale studies of diverse host-guest complexes. Replacing this HF foundation with faster, yet more physically realistic, Density Functional Theory (DFT) approximations can dramatically accelerate the workflow without sacrificing the reliability of the initial search.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully chosen, fast DFT functional (e.g., ωB97X-D or B3LYP-D3(BJ) with a small basis set) can replace HF for the initial conformational sampling and pre-optimization of anion-macrocycle complexes, yielding geometries suitable for high-level refinement while reducing computational cost by over an order of magnitude.
proposals.method: We will develop a tiered computational protocol. First, we will benchmark against known 1:1 and 2:1 anion-bambusuril complexes from your previous studies. Initial conformational searches for new anion guests (e.g., carboxylates, dihydrogen phosphate) will be performed using the GFN2-xTB semiempirical method. Subsequent geometry pre-optimizations will be conducted using selected fast DFT functionals with the 6-31G* basis set, replacing the standard HF/6-31G* step. The performance will be evaluated by comparing the resulting geometries and relative energies to those from the traditional HF-starting point before final refinement with high-level DFT (e.g., DLPNO-CCSD(T) or ωB97X-V/def2-TZVPPD).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, efficient computational protocol specifically tailored for anion receptor screening, enabling the study of larger libraries of hosts and guests. It will directly enhance your group's research on optical receptors and ionic isolation lattices by accelerating the discovery phase. The protocol will be disseminated as a reproducible workflow, contributing a practical tool to the computational supramolecular chemistry community.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is widely used for final energy calculations, its systematic deployment as a *replacement* for HF in the initial, exploratory steps of anion receptor modeling is not standard. The novelty lies in optimizing the cost-accuracy balance specifically for the challenging electrostatic/dispersion-driven binding landscapes of anions with macrocycles, leveraging modern, robust DFT approximations for speed.

proposals.title: Automated High-Throughput Computational Thermochemistry for Anion Binding and Catalysis: A G1-Scale Database Generation Pipeline
proposals.motivation: High-accuracy composite methods like Gaussian-1 (G1) theory provide benchmark thermochemical data crucial for validating lower-cost methods and understanding molecular interactions, such as anion binding by macrocycles or catalytic cycles in CO2 reduction. However, the manual setup, execution, and data extraction for thousands of molecules is prohibitively slow, creating a bottleneck for large-scale screening and database construction in pre-2026 computational chemistry. Automating this process is essential to leverage these accurate methods for materials discovery and mechanistic insight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fully automated computational pipeline, integrating structure generation, job management, error handling, and data parsing, can robustly generate G1-level thermochemical databases (e.g., enthalpies of formation, binding energies) for thousands of anion receptors and catalytic intermediates with minimal human intervention.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based pipeline using the Atomic Simulation Environment (ASE) for initial structure manipulation and connectivity analysis. The core automation will leverage the open-source AutodE package (pre-2026) for systematic conformer generation and DFT-based pre-optimizations (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-SVP). These pre-optimized structures will be fed into a custom job manager that interfaces with Gaussian 16 to execute the multi-step G1 procedure, handling file I/O, error recovery, and restart logic. Final energies will be extracted via cclib, and results stored in a structured SQLite database with associated metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source, robust pipeline for automated G1 calculations, enabling the creation of a large, curated thermochemical database for anion-binding complexes and catalytic intermediates. It will directly accelerate computational screening for novel anion receptors and provide benchmark data to improve machine-learned force fields and lower-level DFT methods in the field.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput DFT screening is common, the full automation of the multi-step, high-accuracy G1 procedure—including error handling for divergent molecular classes—is a novel integration of existing tools. This moves beyond single-point calculations to deliver automated, production-level composite thermochemistry.

proposals.title: Benchmarking G2(MP2/MP3) for Accurate Thermochemistry of Anion-Binding Macrocyclic Complexes
proposals.motivation: High-accuracy composite methods like G2 and G3 are computationally prohibitive for large, flexible supramolecular systems, such as the anion-binding macrocycles and bambusuril-based lattices studied in our group. The recently developed G2(MP2/MP3) method promises near-G2 accuracy at significantly reduced cost, but its performance has not been validated for large, non-covalent complexes where dispersion and conformational flexibility are critical. Establishing its reliability for such systems is essential for enabling predictive computational design of next-generation anion receptors and optical materials.
proposals.hypothesis: The G2(MP2/MP3) composite method will provide thermochemical accuracy (within ~1 kcal/mol) comparable to higher-level benchmarks for anion-macrocycle binding energies and conformational energies, while offering a computational savings of at least 50% in CPU time for systems with 50+ atoms.
proposals.method: We will select a benchmark set of 15-20 complexes from our prior work on anion-macrocycle binding (1:1 and 2:1 stoichiometries) and bambusuril-based ionic lattices. Binding and conformational energies will be computed using G2(MP2/MP3) as implemented in Gaussian 09. Results will be compared against reference values from high-level CCSD(T)/CBS calculations for smaller subsets and against experimental data (where available) for binding affinities. Computational cost (CPU time, disk usage) will be rigorously tracked and compared against standard G2 and G3 calculations for progressively larger systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic validation of G2(MP2/MP3) for large, non-covalent anion-host systems, establishing its domain of applicability for supramolecular chemistry. It will deliver a benchmarked protocol for accurate thermochemical predictions in receptor design, directly impacting the computational study of optical materials and electrocatalytic intermediates where precise energetics are crucial.
proposals.novelty: While G2(MP2/MP3) has been tested on standard thermochemical sets of small molecules, its application to large, flexible supramolecular complexes with significant non-covalent interactions represents a novel and critical stress test, bridging high-accuracy quantum chemistry with practical computational supramolecular chemistry.

proposals.title: Systematic Correction of G1 Thermochemical Errors in Anionic and Electrophilic Species: A Case Study on SO2 and Its Complexes
proposals.motivation: The G1 composite method, while robust for many organic molecules, shows significant and systematic errors for molecules with high electron affinity, such as SO2, where errors in atomization energy can exceed 10 kcal/mol. This inaccuracy undermines the reliability of computational studies on anion binding, atmospheric chemistry, and electrocatalytic cycles involving such problematic species. A targeted investigation is needed to diagnose and correct the specific theoretical failures for this class of molecules.
proposals.hypothesis: The large G1 error for SO2 stems from an inadequate treatment of electron correlation and basis set superposition error (BSSE) in its anionic and highly polar resonance structures, which can be corrected by a tailored, multi-reference-informed composite approach. This correction scheme will be transferable to other problematic electrophilic molecules.
proposals.method: We will perform high-level CCSD(T)/CBS benchmarks for SO2 and related anions (e.g., HSO2-, SO3) to establish reference energies. We will then deconstruct the G1 error, analyzing contributions from basis set incompleteness, core-valence correlation, and higher-order excitations. A corrected composite scheme (G1-SO2) will be developed, potentially incorporating explicitly correlated F12 corrections or multi-reference diagnostics from CASSCF calculations. The method will be validated on a test set of sulfur oxides, halogenated anions, and electrophilic molecules known to challenge G1.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, practical protocol for accurately computing the thermochemistry of problematic, high-electron-affinity molecules critical to anion binding and catalysis. It will provide fundamental insight into the limitations of standard composite methods for systems with significant multi-reference character or diffuse anionic densities. The corrected parameters will enhance the predictive power of computational screening for anion receptors and catalytic cycles involving such species.
proposals.novelty: While G1 errors are known, this proposal uniquely links them to the specific electronic structure features of anion-binding targets and electrophiles, moving beyond generic benchmarking to a chemically motivated correction scheme. It integrates Raghavachari's expertise in composite methods and anion systems to solve a defined accuracy problem.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Experimental Thermochemistry of Second-Row Anions and Complexes with High-Level G1 Theory
proposals.motivation: Accurate thermochemical data for anions and molecular complexes is critical for understanding reaction mechanisms, designing catalysts, and optimizing anion receptors. For second-row species (e.g., S, Cl, P-containing anions and their complexes with organic hosts), experimental data can be sparse or subject to significant uncertainties. A systematic computational validation is needed to identify reliable datasets and highlight potential discrepancies that could misguide molecular design in fields like CO2 reduction and supramolecular chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: The G1 composite quantum chemical method will provide a reliable benchmark to assess and, where necessary, correct experimental thermochemical data (e.g., electron affinities, binding enthalpies) for a diverse set of second-row anions and their host-guest complexes, revealing systematic biases in existing measurements.
proposals.method: We will computationally evaluate a curated set of 50-80 second-row anions and their complexes with model receptors, including species relevant to Dr. Raghavachari's prior work on Mo-hydrides and bambusuril lattices. For each species, we will perform geometry optimization and frequency calculations at the B3LYP/6-31+G(d) level, followed by single-point energy calculations using the Gaussian-1 (G1) composite method as implemented in Gaussian 16. The computed enthalpies of formation, electron affinities, and anion-binding free energies will be directly compared to available experimental data from the NIST CCCBDB and literature. Statistical analysis (mean absolute deviations, error distributions) will quantify the accuracy of the experimental datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated benchmark dataset for second-row anion thermochemistry, directly informing the computational modeling of catalytic cycles and supramolecular assembly. It will provide clear guidance to experimentalists on the reliability of existing measurements and establish a protocol for future data validation. The findings will enhance the predictive power of computational screening for anion-binding receptors and redox-active catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While G1 theory has been used for neutral species benchmarks, its systematic application to validate experimental data for a broad range of second-row anions and their molecular complexes—bridging inorganic catalysis and supramolecular chemistry—is unprecedented. This moves beyond single-molecule studies to provide a landscape assessment of data quality in this chemically important domain.

proposals.title: A Composite Method for Accurate Anion Binding Energies: Bridging Nuclear Physics and Molecular Electronic Structure
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of anion binding energies is critical for designing selective receptors and catalysts, yet remains challenging for electronic structure methods. High-level wavefunction methods are too costly for large host-guest complexes, while density functional theory (DFT) suffers from systematic errors in describing dispersion and charge-transfer interactions. A robust, computationally tractable framework for reliable binding energies is urgently needed in supramolecular and catalytic chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the composite method philosophy, successfully used in nuclear physics to combine different theoretical descriptions of the nucleus, can be adapted to molecular systems by strategically combining multiple electronic structure calculations to cancel systematic errors and yield accurate anion binding energies at reduced cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a molecular composite method by deconstructing the total binding energy into components treated with optimally accurate, cost-effective theories. For a given anion-host complex, we will compute: 1) a high-level core interaction energy (e.g., CCSD(T)) on a truncated model system, 2) a dispersion correction from a DFT-based method (e.g., D4), and 3) a geometry/relaxation energy term from a cost-effective DFT functional. The final binding energy will be a linear combination of these terms, with coefficients and partitioning schemes optimized against a benchmark set of anion binding energies for macrocycles like bambusurils and diimine complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, generally applicable computational protocol for predicting non-covalent interaction energies with near-chemical accuracy at a fraction of the cost of full high-level calculations. It will provide unprecedented insight into the binding landscapes of anion receptors, directly aiding the rational design of next-generation materials for sensing, sequestration, and catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While composite methods (e.g., Gn, CBS) exist in quantum chemistry, the explicit conceptual and structural adaptation of the nuclear physics composite approach—targeting error cancellation by partitioning the physical problem into distinct energy components—for supramolecular anion binding is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: A Composite G1-Level Method for Accurate Energetics of Anion Complexes in Condensed Phases
proposals.motivation: Accurate computational thermochemistry for molecules in solution or solids remains a significant challenge. High-level wavefunction methods like CCSD(T) are prohibitively expensive for large systems, while popular density functionals often lack the required accuracy for non-covalent interactions, especially for anion binding which is critical in catalysis and sensing. A robust, automated composite method with 'chemical accuracy' for condensed phases would bridge this gap, enabling reliable predictions for complex systems like those studied in our group.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite theoretical procedure, analogous to the gas-phase Gaussian-1 (G1) scheme but explicitly incorporating solvation and periodic boundary effects, can be developed to deliver energies for molecular complexes in condensed phases with near-chemical accuracy (±1 kcal/mol) at a tractable computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a layered ONIOM-style approach. The core high-level region (e.g., the anion and primary binding pocket of a receptor) will be treated with a robust, cost-effective composite method (e.g., DLPNO-CCSD(T)/CBS). This will be embedded in an explicit solvent shell or solid-state environment modeled with a cost-effective DFT functional (e.g., ωB97X-D) and a classical polarizable continuum model (PCM). The procedure will be automated via a Python workflow, and parameters (region sizes, embedding schemes) will be optimized against a benchmark set of anion-receptor binding energies in solution and lattice energies of ionic isolation lattices, using available experimental data and high-level calculations as references.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a validated, automated computational protocol for obtaining accurate energetics in condensed phases, specifically tailored for anion-involved processes. It will directly enhance the predictive design of electrocatalysts (like our Mo-complexes) and optical materials (like bambusuril-based fluorophore arrays) by providing reliable solvation and solid-state binding free energies.
proposals.novelty: While composite methods exist for gas-phase thermochemistry, a systematic G1-like scheme parameterized and validated explicitly for condensed-phase environments, particularly for challenging anion binding energetics, is novel. It integrates modern local correlation methods with multi-scale embedding in a standardized, automated workflow.

proposals.title: Quantum Computing-Inspired (QCI) Algorithms for Accurate Excited-State Correlation Energies in Anionic and Charge-Transfer Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of excited-state properties is critical for designing next-generation optical sensors and photocatalysts, particularly for anion-binding receptors and charge-separated complexes. Pre-2026 quantum chemistry methods like EOM-CCSD are accurate but scale poorly, while time-dependent DFT often fails for charge-transfer states. The emerging Quantum Computing-Inspired (QCI) framework, which adapts quantum algorithms for classical hardware, offers a promising route to scalable, high-accuracy correlation methods but has been primarily developed for ground states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a QCI algorithm, specifically designed from the outset to target excited-state wavefunctions via equation-of-motion (EOM) or linear response formalisms, can deliver chemical accuracy for excited-state correlation energies of molecular anions and donor-acceptor complexes at a computational cost scaling polynomially with system size.
proposals.method: We will develop an excited-state QCI (ES-QCI) method by integrating the adaptive sampling configuration interaction (ASCI) core of classical QCI with an EOM framework. The algorithm will use a selected ground-state QCI wavefunction as a reference, from which a subspace for excited states will be iteratively built using perturbation-driven selection of determinants. We will specifically benchmark and optimize the method for challenging cases: excited states of anion-receptor complexes (informed by our group's computational studies) and charge-transfer states in dicationic fluorophore dimers, validating against high-level EOM-CCSD(T) benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first QCI algorithm explicitly optimized for excited states, providing a new computational tool for studying photophysics in supramolecular and catalytic systems. It will establish accuracy benchmarks for excited-state correlation energies of anions and charge-transfer complexes, directly impacting the design of optical anion sensors and photocatalysts for CO2 reduction.
proposals.novelty: While QCI methods exist for ground states, a dedicated excited-state formulation targeting correlation energy accuracy from the outset is novel. The integration of EOM principles with adaptive determinant selection specifically for challenging anionic and charge-separated excitations represents a significant advance beyond time-dependent DFT or standard CI approaches.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Application of QCISD(T) for Accurate Non-Covalent Interactions in Anion-Binding Macrocyclic Receptors
proposals.motivation: The QCISD(T) method is a gold standard for high-accuracy quantum chemical calculations but is computationally prohibitive for chemically relevant systems like anion-binding macrocycles. Pre-2026, its application has been largely confined to small model systems, leaving a gap in benchmark data for larger, functional molecular receptors where dispersion and charge interactions are critical. Validating its performance on these systems is essential to establish reliable reference data for calibrating more efficient DFT and wavefunction methods used in molecular design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the QCISD(T) method, when applied with carefully chosen basis sets and counterpoise corrections, can provide definitive benchmark binding energies for anion-macrocycle complexes, revealing systematic errors in more approximate methods commonly used for receptor design.
proposals.method: We will select a series of anion-macrocycle complexes from Dr. Raghavachari's prior computational landscape study, focusing on 1:1 stoichiometries with anions like Cl− and NO3− and receptors like bambusurils and modified calixarenes. Calculations will be performed using a focal-point approach: geometries will be optimized at the DFT level (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-TZVP), followed by single-point energy evaluations using QCISD(T) with correlation-consistent basis sets (cc-pVDZ, cc-pVTZ) and extrapolation to the complete basis set limit. We will employ local correlation techniques (e.g., PNO-LCCSD(T)) as implemented in programs like ORCA or MRCC to extend the accessible system size, and rigorously account for basis set superposition error.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first set of QCISD(T)-level benchmark binding energies for realistic anion-receptor complexes, providing a critical dataset for the computational supramolecular chemistry community. It will quantitatively assess the accuracy of popular DFT functionals and lower-level wavefunction methods for predicting anion affinity, directly informing the computational design of more selective optical and electrochemical anion sensors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving QCISD(T) from small-model validation to direct application on functional, pre-designed macrocyclic receptors with clear chemical relevance, creating a direct bridge between high-accuracy quantum chemistry and the field of supramolecular anion recognition.

proposals.title: Machine Learning for Accurate and Efficient Prediction of Correlation Energies in Anion-Binding Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of non-covalent interactions, such as anion binding, requires high-level quantum chemical methods like QCISD(T) for reliable correlation energies, but these are computationally prohibitive for large macrocyclic receptors or screening studies. Lower-level DFT or MP2 calculations are faster but lack consistent accuracy. A robust method to predict high-level correlation energies from inexpensive descriptors would dramatically accelerate the computational design of anion receptors and catalysts, a core theme in Raghavachari's group.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on a diverse dataset of anion-macrocycle complexes can accurately predict QCISD-level correlation energies using only features derived from low-level (e.g., DFT or MP2) electronic structure calculations.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset of 1:1 and 2:1 anion-macrocycle complexes from prior work, computing reference QCISD correlation energies and low-level features (e.g., DFT electron densities, orbital energies, partial charges, and geometric descriptors). We will employ kernel ridge regression and graph neural networks (GNNs) as primary models, benchmarking their performance. The models will be trained to predict the difference between the target QCISD correlation energy and a low-level baseline, ensuring transferability across different anion and receptor types.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical computational tool for predicting chemical accuracy at a fraction of the cost, directly applicable to the design of next-generation anion receptors and electrocatalysts. It will provide fundamental insights into which electronic features are most predictive of high-level correlation effects in non-covalent and charge-transfer complexes.
proposals.novelty: While ML for molecular properties is established, a focused model to predict *only* the high-level correlation energy component for anion-binding systems, bridging specific low-cost calculations to QCISD accuracy, is a novel and chemically targeted approach not yet fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Generalized, Parameterized Higher-Level Correction for Accurate Thermochemistry and Non-Covalent Interactions of Anionic and Transition Metal Complexes
proposals.motivation: Composite quantum chemical methods like the correlation consistent Composite Approach (ccCA) and Weizmann-n theories rely on fixed higher-level corrections (HLCs) derived from limited training sets, primarily focused on main-group thermochemistry. This limits their accuracy for critical systems in modern chemistry, such as anion-binding macrocycles, catalytic transition metal complexes, and excitonic assemblies, where non-covalent interactions, charge transfer, and multi-reference character are paramount. A robust, transferable HLC parameterized for this chemical diversity is urgently needed to improve predictive computational design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-parameter HLC, trained on a diverse dataset encompassing main-group and transition metal thermochemistry, anion binding affinities, and intermolecular interaction energies, will yield significantly improved accuracy across these domains compared to existing single-parameter corrections.
proposals.method: We will curate a large, diverse training set (~500 data points) from pre-2026 benchmarks (e.g., GMTKN55, S66, ANION1A, TMC) including bond dissociation energies, reaction barriers, anion binding constants, and interaction energies for supramolecular complexes. The HLC will be formulated as a linear function of system descriptors (e.g., number of paired/unpaired electrons, formal metal charge, halogen count). Parameters will be optimized via linear regression against high-level reference data (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS) using robust fitting techniques to prevent overfitting. Validation will be performed on held-out test sets of anion receptors and Mo/W catalysts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, parameterized HLC scheme that can be integrated into standard composite methods, dramatically improving their accuracy for anion supramolecular chemistry and transition metal catalysis. It will provide the computational chemistry community with a more reliable tool for predicting binding constants and reaction energetics in complex molecular systems.
proposals.novelty: While HLCs exist, none are systematically parameterized for the combined challenge of anion-involving non-covalent interactions and transition metal chemistry, moving beyond the traditional focus on main-group atomization energies.

proposals.title: A Fragmentation-Based G3 Composite Method for High-Accuracy Thermochemistry of Anion-Binding Biomolecular Complexes
proposals.motivation: The G3 composite method provides benchmark thermochemical accuracy for small molecules but is computationally intractable for large systems like proteins or DNA. Understanding the precise binding energetics of anions to biological receptors is crucial for designing sensors and catalysts, yet current density functional theory (DFT) methods lack the required accuracy. A scalable, high-accuracy quantum chemical approach is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the G3 composite energy of a large biomolecular system can be accurately reconstructed from the G3 energies of smaller, overlapping fragments, enabling the calculation of benchmark-quality anion-binding affinities for macrocyclic receptors and their biomolecular analogues.
proposals.method: We will adapt the G3(MP2) composite model for use with the systematic molecular fragmentation (SMF) approach. The target protein or DNA-anion complex will be partitioned into capped fragments. G3(MP2) single-point energies will be computed for each fragment and for overlapping dimers to account for inter-fragment correlations. The total G3-level energy will be assembled using the SMF many-body expansion, with long-range electrostatic effects incorporated via an embedded charge scheme. Validation will be performed on smaller anion-macrocycle complexes where full G3 calculations are feasible.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first software framework for applying G3-level accuracy to systems with hundreds of atoms, directly enabling high-fidelity studies of anion binding in biological environments. It will provide benchmark data to critically assess DFT functionals for biomolecular anionics. The method will be a powerful new tool for the computational design of optical anion receptors and bio-inspired catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While fragmentation methods exist for DFT and MP2, applying a multi-step, high-level composite method like G3 through a fragmentation framework is unprecedented. The integration specifically targets the accurate description of anion-binding motifs, combining Dr. Raghavachari's expertise in composite methods and anion complexation.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Ab Initio/DFT Protocol for Accurate and Efficient Modeling of Anion Binding Energetics in Macrocyclic Receptors
proposals.motivation: Accurate computational prediction of anion binding affinities is crucial for designing selective receptors, but high-level ab initio methods like CCSD(T) are prohibitively expensive for large host-guest complexes. Conversely, standard density functional theory (DFT) often suffers from systematic errors in describing non-covalent interactions, especially dispersion and anion-π interactions. A robust, lower-cost computational protocol is needed to bridge this accuracy-efficiency gap for supramolecular design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically replacing the most computationally demanding components of a high-level ab initio workflow with carefully validated DFT functionals, we can achieve chemical accuracy (within ~1 kcal/mol) for anion binding energies at a fraction of the computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid protocol where the geometry optimization and frequency calculations for the host, guest, and complex are performed using a dispersion-corrected DFT functional (e.g., ωB97X-D). The single-point electronic energy for the binding reaction will then be computed using a composite method: the large macrocyclic system's energy will be calculated with a cost-effective double-hybrid DFT functional (e.g., DSD-PBEP86), while the smaller anion's correlation energy will be refined using a localized MP2 or CCSD(T) calculation on the anion and its immediate binding pocket, effectively creating an embedded high-level correction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source computational workflow for supramolecular chemists to predict anion binding constants with high confidence. It will provide critical benchmarks on the performance of double-hybrid DFT for anion-macrocycle interactions and establish guidelines for when embedded high-level corrections are necessary.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in using DFT alone, but in the systematic decomposition of the binding energy calculation and the targeted application of high-level methods only where they are most needed, creating a novel multi-level embedding scheme tailored for large, flexible anion receptors.

proposals.title: Multi-Reference Ab Initio Characterization of Anion-Binding Complexes with Strong Static Correlation
proposals.motivation: Single-reference quantum chemical methods like MP4 or CCSD(T) can fail for systems with significant multiconfigurational character, such as open-shell transition states, diradicals, or complexes with strong charge-transfer interactions. In anion-binding chemistry, certain host-guest complexes, especially those involving redox-active or highly polarizable receptors and anions, may exhibit such problematic electronic structures, leading to unreliable predictions of binding energies and electronic spectra.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying multi-configurational self-consistent field (MCSCF) methods and subsequent multireference perturbation theory (e.g., CASPT2, NEVPT2) will provide quantitatively accurate and qualitatively correct descriptions of binding energies and excited states for a subset of anion-receptor complexes where single-reference methods are demonstrably inadequate.
proposals.method: We will select problematic cases from our prior computational survey of macrocyclic anion receptors, focusing on complexes with suspected strong static correlation. For these, we will perform complete active space self-consistent field (CASSCF) calculations to treat near-degeneracies, followed by dynamic correlation correction using N-electron valence state perturbation theory (NEVPT2). The active space will be carefully selected based on orbital entropy analysis from preliminary density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations. Binding energies, orbital compositions, and excited states (for optical properties) will be computed and compared directly to single-reference (e.g., CCSD(T), MP4) and density functional theory results.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust multi-reference protocol for accurately modeling challenging anion-binding systems, moving beyond the limitations of standard DFT and single-reference wavefunction theory. It will provide benchmark-quality data for a new class of complexes, guiding the design of next-generation anion receptors with tailored electronic and optical properties. The study will also clarify the specific electronic structure features that necessitate a multiconfigurational approach in supramolecular chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While multi-reference methods are standard in inorganic and theoretical chemistry, their systematic application to benchmark and correct the description of anion-binding energetics and spectroscopy in supramolecular systems is novel. This bridges a gap between high-accuracy electronic structure theory and the practical computational design of functional molecular receptors.

proposals.title: Basis Set Optimization for G3(MP2) Theory: Enhancing Accuracy for Anion and Transition Metal Complex Thermochemistry
proposals.motivation: The G3(MP2) composite method is a widely used, cost-effective approach for predicting thermochemical properties. However, its standard basis sets (e.g., 6-31G(d) for geometries, 6-311+G(3df,2p) for final energies) were adopted from earlier Gaussian-n theories and have not been systematically re-optimized for the specific components of the G3(MP2) scheme. This can lead to suboptimal accuracy, particularly for challenging systems like anions and transition metal complexes where electron correlation and basis set effects are critical, as highlighted in Raghavachari's prior work on anion binding and electrocatalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by re-optimizing the basis sets used in each step of the G3(MP2) procedure (geometry optimization, zero-point energy, and correlation energy contributions) against a high-accuracy benchmark, we can significantly improve the method's predictive accuracy for formation enthalpies, electron affinities, and binding energies, especially for anions and metal-organic complexes, without increasing computational cost.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step optimization protocol. First, a training set of ~100 molecules, including anions, organic species, and transition metal complexes relevant to catalysis and binding, will be assembled using high-level reference data (e.g., W4, ATcT). Using automated scripts, we will systematically vary the exponents and contraction coefficients of the basis sets used in key G3(MP2) steps—specifically the MP2/GTMP2Large and QCISD(T)/G3Large steps—via even-tempered expansions. Optimization will be performed using a genetic algorithm to minimize the root-mean-square error (RMSE) against the benchmark thermochemistry. The performance of the new basis sets will be rigorously validated on a separate test set.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, publicly available set of optimized basis functions for G3(MP2), leading to a more accurate and reliable variant of this popular method. It will directly benefit computational studies in anion recognition and molecular catalysis by providing improved, cost-effective predictions for binding energies and reaction energetics. The project establishes a framework for basis set optimization tailored to composite chemistry methods.
proposals.novelty: While basis set optimization is common for individual *ab initio* methods, a targeted optimization of the disparate basis sets within a established, multi-step composite scheme like G3(MP2) is novel. This approach moves beyond using 'off-the-shelf' basis sets to create a unified set specifically designed for the method's architecture.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Anion Receptors with Target Binding Energies Using a G3(MP2)-Constrained Genetic Algorithm
proposals.motivation: The rational design of synthetic anion receptors, crucial for sensing and catalysis, remains challenging due to the difficulty of predicting host-guest binding energies with the accuracy required for experimental validation. While high-level composite methods like G3(MP2) provide benchmark thermochemical accuracy (~1 kcal/mol), they are computationally prohibitive for broad molecular exploration. An inverse design framework that directly targets G3(MP2)-level accuracy for candidate molecules would bridge the gap between computational discovery and experimental synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a G3(MP2) energy calculation as a final-stage validation filter within a genetic algorithm (GA) driven by faster, machine-learned potentials will enable the efficient discovery of novel anion-binding macrocycles with predictable, high-accuracy binding affinities.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage inverse design pipeline. First, a GA will evolve molecular graphs of macrocyclic scaffolds, using a pre-trained neural network potential (e.g., ANI-2x or a custom SchNet model) for rapid fitness evaluation based on target binding energy and synthetic accessibility. Second, the top candidate structures from the GA will be subjected to single-point G3(MP2) energy calculations on pre-optimized DFT geometries. The G3(MP2)-computed binding energies will serve as the final constraint; molecules meeting the target energy window will be output as designed receptors. The workflow will be validated on known anion complexes from the researcher's prior computational landscape studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational protocol for generating chemically realistic anion receptors with quantifiable, high-accuracy binding strengths. It will yield specific, synthetically accessible macrocycle designs for anions like chloride or phosphate, providing testable predictions for collaborators. The method establishes a paradigm for using gold-standard quantum chemistry as a direct constraint in generative molecular design.
proposals.novelty: While inverse design and genetic algorithms are established, using G3(MP2)—a high-accuracy, computationally expensive composite method—as an explicit constraint and validation step within the design loop is a novel strategy to ensure discovery credibility beyond standard DFT-level approaches.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Anion Binding Affinities in Large, Functional Molecular Systems: A Combined Computational and Experimental Test Set
proposals.motivation: Computational methods for predicting anion binding, such as density functional theory (DFT), are typically benchmarked on small, idealized host-guest complexes. However, the field's push towards functional systems—like the electrocatalytic Mo-complexes and extended chromophores studied in our group—demands validation on larger, more structurally diverse molecules where long-range interactions, conformational flexibility, and solvent effects are critical. Current test sets lack the size and complexity of these real-world targets, creating a gap between computational validation and practical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the benchmark test set to include larger, functionally relevant molecular systems will reveal significant deviations in the performance of standard computational methods (e.g., DFT functionals, dispersion corrections) compared to their established accuracy for smaller models, thereby identifying specific limitations for modeling complex anion interactions.
proposals.method: We will curate a new test set of 20-25 large molecular systems (MW > 500 Da) from recent literature and our group's work, including expanded bambusuril analogs, dicationic fluorophore receptors, and redox-active transition metal complexes. High-level DLPNO-CCSD(T) calculations will provide reference binding energies for a subset of smaller motifs. For the full set, we will perform systematic benchmarking using a panel of DFT functionals (e.g., ωB97X-D, B3LYP-D3, M06-2X) with implicit and explicit solvation models (SMD, COSMO-RS). Binding affinities for select synthetic targets will be experimentally determined via isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and optical anion-binding assays to provide ground-truth validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available benchmark set for anion binding in large, functionally complex molecules, directly linking fundamental computational validation to applied molecular design. It will provide concrete guidelines for computational chemists and supramolecular researchers on method selection for modeling anions in catalytic, sensing, and separation-relevant systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving benchmark sets beyond simple model complexes to encompass the scale and multifunctionality of modern anion receptor chemistry, explicitly testing computational methods under conditions relevant to contemporary research in catalysis and photophysics.

proposals.title: Adaptive Multi-Level Computational Protocol for Anion Binding Affinity Prediction in Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurate computational prediction of anion binding affinities is crucial for designing receptors for sensing, catalysis, and environmental remediation. However, high-level quantum chemical methods like CCSD(T) are computationally prohibitive for large, flexible supramolecular systems, while faster DFT methods can suffer from systematic errors. A one-size-fits-all computational approach is inefficient and limits the study of chemically relevant, complex anion-receptor assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive computational protocol, which automatically selects and applies an appropriate level of theory based on real-time metrics of molecular complexity and electronic structure, can achieve accuracy comparable to high-level benchmarks at a fraction of the computational cost for diverse anion-binding complexes.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision-tree protocol that begins with a low-cost DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D) geometry optimization and initial energy calculation. Key complexity descriptors (e.g., system size, conformational flexibility, charge distribution, presence of heavy elements) will be computed. Based on these, the protocol will adaptively route calculations: for simple, rigid complexes, it will proceed directly to high-level DLPNO-CCSD(T) single-point corrections; for larger, flexible systems, it will first employ more robust DFT functionals or embedded QM/MM schemes before deciding on the necessity of a wavefunction-based correction. The routing logic will be trained and validated on a curated benchmark set of anion complexes with known experimental binding energies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source computational workflow that dramatically extends the size and complexity of anion-binding systems that can be studied with reliable accuracy. It will provide specific guidelines for cost-effective theory selection in supramolecular chemistry, directly supporting the design of next-generation anion receptors and catalysts.
proposals.novelty: While multi-level schemes exist, a fully adaptive protocol that makes runtime decisions based on quantitative molecular complexity metrics, specifically tailored for the challenging electronic structure of anion complexes, is a novel and non-obvious advancement beyond static composite methods.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the XYG3 Double-Hybrid Functional for Anion-Responsive Excited States: A Linear Response TD-DFT Study for Optical Sensors
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of electronic excitation energies is critical for designing optical anion receptors and understanding photophysical processes in complexes, as highlighted by your recent work on anion binding and exciton coupling. The XYG3 double-hybrid functional has shown excellent performance for ground-state thermochemistry and non-covalent interactions, but its systematic evaluation for excited states via time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) remains limited, especially for systems where anion binding induces significant electronic changes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the XYG3 functional, due to its incorporation of exact Hartree-Fock exchange and second-order perturbation theory, will provide superior accuracy for low-lying valence and charge-transfer excitation energies in anion-binding chromophores compared to standard hybrid functionals, offering a reliable yet computationally efficient tool for optical sensor design.
proposals.method: We will perform linear-response TD-DFT calculations using the XYG3 functional on a curated benchmark set of organic and organometallic chromophores with known anion-binding affinity, including derivatives of your studied bambusuril and macrocyclic systems. Excitation energies for the first few singlet and triplet states will be computed and compared against high-level reference data (e.g., CC2, CASPT2) and experimental UV-vis spectra. The assessment will quantify errors for local, Rydberg, and charge-transfer excitations, with specific analysis of how anion complexation shifts predicted excitations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive benchmark of XYG3 for excited-state properties relevant to anion sensing, providing clear guidelines for its use in photochemical studies of supramolecular complexes. It will deliver a validated, accurate computational protocol to aid in the rational design of next-generation optical anion receptors and fluorophores.
proposals.novelty: While XYG3 is recognized for ground-state accuracy, its systematic TD-DFT performance for the critical yet challenging excitations in anion-binding scaffolds is unexplored, merging advanced density functional development with applied computational supramolecular photochemistry.

proposals.title: Empirical Corrections to G2 Theory for Accurate Thermochemistry of Polyhalogenated Molecules
proposals.motivation: G2 theory and its variants are widely used for accurate thermochemical predictions, but they are known to exhibit systematic errors for molecules containing multiple fluorine or chlorine atoms, such as perfluorinated hydrocarbons and chlorinated solvents. These errors, often exceeding 5 kcal/mol, limit the reliability of computational design in areas like anion receptor chemistry, catalysis, and environmental science where halogenated species are crucial. Addressing this deficiency is essential for predictive computational modeling in these fields.
proposals.hypothesis: The large errors in G2 theory for polyhalogenated molecules arise from deficiencies in the treatment of electron correlation and basis set superposition error (BSSE) for highly electronegative atoms, which can be corrected by introducing molecule-specific, parameterized empirical terms based on halogen count and bonding environment.
proposals.method: We will first compile a benchmark dataset of experimental formation enthalpies for 30-40 molecules with 2+ F/Cl atoms, including species relevant to anion binding (e.g., C2F6, SF6, Cl2C=CH2). High-level CCSD(T)/CBS calculations will provide reference energies. We will then perform standard G2 calculations, analyze error trends, and develop a multi-parameter correction function. This function will incorporate terms for the number of halogen atoms, their hybridization (sp3 vs sp2), and a BSSE-like estimate using counterpoise corrections on halogenated fragments. The parameters will be optimized via linear regression against the reference dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a modified, publicly available G2 procedure (G2-X) with significantly improved accuracy (<1 kcal/mol mean absolute error) for polyhalogenated systems. It will directly benefit computational studies of halogen bonding, anion receptor design, and environmental degradation pathways. The parameterization strategy will also provide insight into the physical origins of the errors in composite methods.
proposals.novelty: While empirical corrections exist for general G2 errors, a targeted, chemically intuitive correction scheme based on halogen count and bonding motifs for polyhalogenated molecules has not been systematically developed and validated against a high-level benchmark set.

proposals.title: Systematic Benchmarking and Extension of the G2(MP2,SVP) Composite Method for Anion Binding, Transition Metal Complexes, and Excited-State Fluorophores
proposals.motivation: The G2(MP2,SVP) composite method offers a promising balance of accuracy and computational cost for thermochemical predictions, but its validation has been largely confined to neutral, closed-shell organic molecules and main-group species. Pre-2026, there is a critical need for reliable, cost-effective computational methods to model complex systems like anion-receptor complexes, catalytic transition metal intermediates, and photoactive organic ions, which are central to modern energy and sensing applications. A systematic evaluation of G2(MP2,SVP) across these challenging chemical spaces is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the G2(MP2,SVP) composite method, with potential minor modifications to its empirical higher-level correction (HLC), can achieve chemical accuracy (within 1 kcal/mol) for binding energies of anion-macrocycle complexes, reaction energies of small-molecule activation at Mo centers, and vertical excitation energies of dicationic fluorophores.
proposals.method: We will curate benchmark datasets from literature and high-level CCSD(T)/CBS calculations for three classes: (1) anion binding energies with macrocyclic receptors (e.g., bambusurils), (2) key intermediates in the electrocatalytic CO2 reduction cycle by Mo-diimine complexes, and (3) low-lying excited states of ionic fluorophores. The standard G2(MP2,SVP) protocol will be applied using Gaussian 16. Statistical error analysis (MAE, RMSE) will be performed. If systematic biases are found, we will explore re-parameterizing the HLC using a subset of the new data to create class-specific corrections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive accuracy assessment of G2(MP2,SVP) for anion-involving, transition metal-containing, and charged excited-state systems, delivering clear guidelines for its reliable application. It will yield either validated protocols or new, class-specific parameterizations, enabling more accurate and accessible computational studies in catalysis, supramolecular chemistry, and photophysics.
proposals.novelty: While G2 methods are well-known, their rigorous testing and potential adaptation for the precise calculation of anion binding affinities and properties of charged, open-shell transition metal species relevant to catalysis and photonics is novel and directly bridges Raghavachari's expertise in composite methods with his recent research themes.

proposals.title: Accurate Enthalpy Calculations for Large Anion-Binding Clusters via Plane-Wave Density Functional Theory in a Periodic Boundary Framework
proposals.motivation: Accurately calculating binding enthalpies for large, flexible molecular clusters, such as anion-receptor complexes and supramolecular assemblies, remains a significant challenge for traditional Gaussian-basis-set quantum chemistry due to steep computational scaling. Plane-wave DFT with periodic boundary conditions (PBC), the standard for solid-state physics, offers superior computational efficiency and systematic improvability for large, periodic systems, but its direct application to isolated, non-covalent molecular clusters is underexplored and requires careful methodological adaptation to avoid spurious interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by employing a rigorously benchmarked supercell approach with explicit corrections for finite-size effects, plane-wave PBC-DFT can yield accurate binding enthalpies for large molecular anion complexes (e.g., 1:1 and 2:1 macrocycle-anion clusters) at a computational cost significantly lower than conventional correlated wavefunction methods.
proposals.method: We will employ the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) to perform plane-wave PBC-DFT calculations. Large, neutral molecular clusters (e.g., bambusuril-anion complexes) will be placed in a cubic supercell with sufficient vacuum (>15 Å) to minimize image interactions. We will systematically benchmark exchange-correlation functionals (PBE, PBE-D3, SCAN, rVV10) against high-level CCSD(T) or DLPNO-CCSD(T) benchmarks on smaller model systems. Enthalpies will be computed via supercell energy differences, with corrections applied for basis set superposition error (BSSE) using the counterpoise method adapted for PBC and for long-range electrostatic interactions using the Makov-Payne or modern SCDFT corrections.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, efficient protocol for applying solid-state DFT codes to molecular cluster thermodynamics, directly benefiting the computational study of supramolecular and anion-binding chemistry. It will provide new, accurate benchmark data for the binding strengths in large, complex anion receptors, informing the design of next-generation optical sensors and sequestration agents.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored transfer of solid-state periodic DFT methodology to the specific problem of enthalpy calculation for discrete, neutral molecular clusters prevalent in supramolecular chemistry, moving beyond its traditional use in crystalline solids or charged systems, and rigorously validating it for anion-binding applications central to the researcher's expertise.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Quantum Chemical Methods for Anion Binding and Optical Properties of Large Bio-Relevant Molecules and Receptors
proposals.motivation: Computational studies of anion binding, like those on macrocyclic receptors, are crucial for designing sensors and catalysts, but benchmarking has been largely confined to small model systems. To guide the rational design of anion receptors for biological applications—such as targeting phosphate or carboxylate groups on drug candidates or biomolecules—the accuracy of density functional theory (DFT) and other methods must be systematically assessed for large, flexible, and functionally relevant molecular scaffolds. This is a critical, unmet need in pre-2026 computational chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative performance rankings of common DFT functionals and dispersion corrections, established for small anion complexes, will change significantly when applied to large, conformationally flexible organic and biomolecular receptors due to the increased importance of long-range dispersion, solvation effects, and conformational sampling.
proposals.method: We will adapt the computational benchmarking methodology from our prior work on macrocyclic anion receptors to a curated set of 20-30 large targets, including drug-like molecules with anion-binding pharmacophores, extended bambusuril analogs, and known protein-binding motifs.
