Binding energies for biologically relevant anions (e.g., phosphate, carboxylate) will be computed using a hierarchy of methods: high-level DLPNO-CCSD(T) as a reference, a range of DFT functionals (e.g., ωB97X-D, B3LYP-D3, M06-2X), and semi-empirical methods for initial screening. Conformational ensembles will be generated using CREST, and solvation (PCM, SMD) will be integrated. Optical property (absorption/emission) predictions for fluorophore-tagged receptors will also be benchmarked against available experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available benchmark dataset for anion binding to large bio-relevant molecules, providing validated protocol recommendations for computational drug and sensor design. It will directly extend the PI's expertise in anion binding and optical receptors to the biologically critical scale, bridging computational inorganic/physical chemistry with medicinal and bio-organic chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks for small molecule binding are common, a systematic assessment focused on the accuracy of quantum chemical methods for anion interactions with large, flexible drug-like molecules and synthetic receptors is novel and addresses a specific gap between computational methodology and practical application in pre-2026 biomolecular design.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Computational Thermochemistry: Automated Workflows for Accurate Formation Enthalpies of Anion Receptors and Catalytic Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurate gas-phase formation enthalpies are critical for predicting binding affinities, reaction energies, and stability in molecular design, particularly for anion receptors and transition metal catalysts. However, high-accuracy composite methods like G2(MP2,SVP) are computationally prohibitive for large-scale screening. This bottleneck limits the exploration of chemical space for designing next-generation materials and catalysts, such as those for CO2 reduction or anion sequestration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing an automated, robust computational workflow, we can efficiently and reliably apply high-accuracy thermochemical methods to thousands of molecules, enabling the identification of structure-property relationships and novel leads that are inaccessible via lower-level methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based workflow manager using the Atomic Simulation Environment (ASE) to automate the complete thermochemical pipeline. This includes geometry optimization and frequency calculations at the DFT level (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-SVP), followed by single-point energy calculations using the G2(MP2,SVP) composite method via the Gaussian 16 software. The workflow will handle error checking, job submission on high-performance computing clusters, and data aggregation into a structured database. Initial validation will be performed on a curated set of known macrocyclic anion hosts and mononuclear Mo complexes from the PI's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, validated software framework for high-throughput thermochemistry and a foundational dataset of accurate formation enthalpies for hundreds of anion-binding and catalytic motifs. It will establish benchmarks for the accuracy-efficiency trade-off in large-scale screening and provide concrete design rules for stronger anion receptors and more stable catalytic intermediates.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput DFT screening is common, the systematic automation of high-level composite methods for large, diverse molecular sets is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the gap between benchmark-quality accuracy and the scale required for genuine discovery in supramolecular and inorganic chemistry.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Extending the G4 Composite Method for Accurate Thermochemistry of Anion-Binding Macrocyclic Complexes
proposals.motivation: The G4 composite method provides exceptional accuracy for thermochemical properties of small molecules, but its application to larger, functional systems like anion-binding macrocycles and their host-guest complexes remains largely untested. As computational design of selective anion receptors advances, there is a critical need for benchmark-quality reference data to validate more efficient DFT methods used for screening. This gap limits the reliable prediction of binding affinities and stabilities for complex supramolecular systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the G4 composite method can be systematically applied to macrocyclic anion receptors and their 1:1 and 2:1 complexes with anions to generate a benchmark dataset, revealing that its accuracy remains high for these larger systems dominated by electrostatic and dispersion interactions.
proposals.method: We will select a diverse set of macrocyclic receptors (e.g., bambusurils, modified calixarenes) and anions (Cl-, Br-, NO3-, OAc-) from recent literature. For each host, guest, and their complexes, we will perform full G4 calculations to determine binding enthalpies and free energies. The computational workflow will use Gaussian 09 with the standard G4 recipe, employing efficient molecular fragmentation and conformational search protocols for the larger systems. Results will be compared against available experimental binding data and lower-level DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-TZVP) calculations to assess performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-accuracy benchmark dataset for anion-macrocycle binding energies, providing a critical reference for the computational supramolecular chemistry community. It will establish the practical scope and limitations of G4 for non-covalent complexes and guide the parameterization of more cost-effective DFT methods for receptor design.
proposals.novelty: While G4 is well-established for small molecules, its rigorous application to large, flexible macrocycles and their anion complexes is novel. The study uniquely bridges high-accuracy quantum chemistry with the practical demands of supramolecular anion receptor design, moving beyond small test sets to functionally relevant systems.

proposals.title: A Physically Derived, System-Specific Correction Scheme for Anion Binding Energies: Moving Beyond Empirical Atom-Pair Potentials
proposals.motivation: Accurate computational modeling of anion binding is crucial for designing receptors and catalysts, but widely used density functional theory (DFT) methods suffer from systematic errors. The DFT-D3 and related empirical dispersion corrections with fixed, atom-pairwise parameters are not transferable across diverse chemical environments, particularly for large, polarizable anions and macrocyclic hosts where many-body dispersion and local polarization effects are significant. This lack of a physically motivated, system-specific correction limits predictive accuracy in the study of anion complexes, a core theme of Raghavachari's research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the fixed higher-level correction scheme with a model that derives dispersion and short-range correlation corrections from the system's instantaneous electron density will yield more accurate and transferable binding energies for anion-host complexes across varying stoichiometries and geometries.
proposals.method: We will develop and implement a machine-learned, density-dependent correction model. Training data will be generated using high-level DLPNO-CCSD(T) calculations on a diverse set of anion-macrocycle complexes (1:1 and 2:1) from Raghavachari's prior work. The model will use a neural network architecture that takes local descriptors of the electron density (e.g., from a symmetry-adapted atomic density grid) as input to predict a system-specific, non-pairwise additive correction energy. This model will be integrated into a DFT workflow and validated against benchmark sets of anion binding affinities and optical properties of receptor-fluorophore assemblies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool for the molecular design of anion receptors and catalysts by delivering a more reliable and physically grounded method for calculating intermolecular interactions. It will advance the field by directly linking correction energy to the electronic structure, moving beyond parametric fits. The resulting code and benchmark data will be a significant resource for the computational chemistry community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in abandoning the fixed atom-pairwise correction paradigm in favor of a machine-learned, electron-density-derived model that inherently captures many-body and environment-specific effects for anion binding, a direct challenge to the empirical correction schemes standard in pre-2026 computational chemistry.

proposals.title: Adaptive, Hierarchical Composite Methods for High-Accuracy Anion and Complex Binding Energies
proposals.motivation: The CCSD(T)/CBS composite approach is the pre-2026 gold standard for thermochemistry but has known limitations for systems with significant multi-reference character, such as transition metal complexes and anion-receptor systems. Furthermore, its fixed, linear hierarchy of basis sets and single correlation treatment is computationally inefficient and cannot systematically surpass the CCSD(T) approximation. A more flexible, system-adaptive framework is needed to push accuracy boundaries for challenging molecular interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive composite method, which uses low-cost diagnostics to select and optimally combine post-CCSD(T) correlation treatments (e.g., CCSDT, CCSDT(Q), DCSD) with targeted basis sets, will yield more accurate and computationally efficient predictions of binding energies for anions and molecular complexes than the rigid CCSD(T)/CBS paradigm.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision framework based on pre-2026 diagnostics: T1 and D1 diagnostics for multi-reference character, %TAE[(T)] for importance of connected triples, and basis set convergence profiles from MP2 or DLPNO-CCSD(T). This framework will route systems to tailored composite models. For example, systems with high multi-reference character will use a composite of CASSCF/NEVPT2 for the active space and DCSD for dynamic correlation, each with its own optimized basis set sequence. The computational workflow will be automated using extensions of existing scripting in Molpro and CFOUR.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, open-source software protocol for adaptive high-accuracy quantum chemistry, moving beyond the one-size-fits-all CCSD(T)/CBS model. It will provide benchmark-quality binding energies for challenging anion-macrocycle and catalytic metal-hydride complexes, directly informing the design of new receptors and catalysts.
proposals.novelty: The approach fundamentally inverts the traditional composite method philosophy by making the high-level theory selection and basis set path contingent on system-specific diagnostics, rather than applying a fixed hierarchy. This creates a pathway to systematically exceed CCSD(T) accuracy without universal application of prohibitively expensive methods like CCSDT(Q).

proposals.title: Adaptive Surrogate-Model-Driven Optimization of Anion-Binding Complexes Using High-Level Electronic Structure Theory
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of the structure and binding energies of anion-macrocycle complexes is crucial for designing selective receptors, but geometry optimization at high-level quantum chemical methods (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS) is computationally prohibitive. Lower-level methods like DFT are efficient but often unreliable for these non-covalent systems, leading to significant errors in predicted geometries and energetics. A robust, cost-effective strategy to locate high-level minima is therefore a critical unmet need in computational anion recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive optimization workflow, which uses a fast DFT surface for exploration and corrects its gradients with a locally trained surrogate model informed by sparse high-level single-point calculations, will converge to geometries and energies statistically indistinguishable from a full high-level optimization at a small fraction of the computational cost.
proposals.method: We will implement a workflow where initial optimization occurs on a DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D) potential energy surface. At strategic points (e.g., based on gradient norm or energy change), we will perform single-point calculations using a high-level method like DLPNO-CCSD(T). These high-fidelity data will train a local Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate model to predict corrections to the DFT energy and gradients. The optimization (using a quasi-Newton algorithm) will then proceed on the GP-corrected surface, with the training set adaptively expanded until convergence criteria on the high-level energy are met.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical computational protocol for obtaining reliable high-level geometries of supramolecular complexes, specifically applied to challenging anion-binding systems. It will provide new benchmarks for the structures of 1:1 and 2:1 anion-macrocycle complexes, directly complementing our group's prior computational landscape studies. The method itself will be a general contribution to computational chemistry, enabling high-accuracy studies of molecular systems previously considered too large.
proposals.novelty: While surrogate models exist for global potential energy surfaces, the novelty lies in the adaptive, local, and gradient-aware integration of the surrogate directly into the optimization loop, specifically tailored for the nuanced energy landscapes of anion-receptor complexes where multiple minima and subtle interactions are paramount.

proposals.title: A Self-Consistent, Geometry-Aware Density Functional for Accurate Molecular Structures and Anion Binding Energies
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of molecular geometries, vibrational frequencies, and non-covalent binding energies is crucial for designing anion receptors and catalysts, but remains a challenge for density functional theory (DFT). While composite methods like G4 offer high accuracy, they are computationally prohibitive for geometry optimizations of large systems. Current dual-level schemes (e.g., ONIOM) or single-point corrections on DFT geometries are approximations that can fail for flexible systems or along reaction paths.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a density functional whose parameters are dynamically optimized during the geometry search to match the energy and gradient of a high-level composite method will inherently produce molecular structures and properties with near-composite accuracy at DFT cost.
proposals.method: We will develop an iterative algorithm where, at each step of a geometry optimization, a parameterized meta-GGA functional (e.g., a modified B97M-V) is evaluated. Its parameters will be adjusted via a machine learning regression (e.g., Bayesian optimization) to minimize the difference between its energy/gradient and a target G4(MP2) energy/gradient for the current nuclear configuration. This process will be embedded within a quasi-Newton optimizer (e.g., BFGS) to achieve self-consistency between the functional form, its parameters, and the final geometry. The method will be validated on benchmark sets for anion-molecule complexes, macrocycle geometries, and reaction paths for CO2 reduction catalysts.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel DFT framework that directly yields composite-level geometries and frequencies, enabling high-accuracy studies of large, flexible anion-binding systems and catalytic intermediates. It will provide the computational chemistry community with a practical tool that bridges the accuracy-cost gap for structural predictions, moving beyond post-hoc corrections.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing parameterized functionals fitted to static databases, our functional's parameters are dynamically and uniquely determined by the optimization path of the specific molecule, creating a bespoke, geometry-optimized functional. This fusion of the electronic structure and optimization problems is a fundamental departure from standard DFT workflows.

proposals.title: Active Learning-Driven G4 Error Correction for Accurate Thermochemistry of Anion-Binding Complexes and Catalysts
proposals.motivation: The high-accuracy G4 composite method is computationally prohibitive for large systems like anion-binding macrocycles and transition metal catalysts, limiting its predictive power for binding energies and reaction barriers. While machine learning (ML) models for G4 error prediction exist, they are static and trained on small, generic molecules, making their transferability to complex, functional molecular systems uncertain. An active learning framework is needed to build reliable, system-specific error models that make high-level theory applicable to the large, charged, and metal-containing systems central to modern molecular design.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning loop, which strategically selects large, complex molecules for full G4 calculation based on ML model uncertainty, will generate a self-improving error correction model that maintains G4-level accuracy for large anion receptors and organometallic complexes at a fraction of the computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop a Python-based active learning workflow. An initial ML model (e.g., a Gaussian Process Regressor or a graph neural network) will be trained on pre-2026 G4 error data for small molecules. For a target large system (e.g., a bambusuril-anion complex or Mo-diimine catalyst), a fast, tentative G4 calculation will be performed, and its features used to query the ML model's predictive uncertainty. Systems with high uncertainty will be prioritized for full, careful G4 analysis. These new, high-value data points will be added to the training set to iteratively retrain and refine the error model, expanding its domain to larger, more relevant chemical spaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical software framework and validated protocol for applying G4-level accuracy to large molecular systems, specifically targeting anion-binding and catalytic complexes. It will provide new, benchmark-quality thermochemical data for these systems and demonstrate a paradigm for cost-effective, high-accuracy computational screening and design. The resulting active error model will be a transferable tool for the computational chemistry community.
proposals.novelty: The integration of active learning directly into the G4 workflow for error prediction is novel, moving beyond static ML correction. Its strategic focus on expanding the model's domain to chemically relevant, large, and complex systems—rather than just improving accuracy on small molecules—addresses a critical gap in making high-level theory practically useful for molecular design.

proposals.title: An Adaptive Gaussian-5 Composite Method with On-the-Fly Multi-Reference Diagnostics for Accurate Thermochemistry of Anion Complexes and Open-Shell Systems
proposals.motivation: High-accuracy composite methods like G4 and G5 are essential for reliable thermochemical predictions in molecular design, but they fail catastrophically for systems with strong multi-reference character, such as diradicals, transition states, and open-shell transition metal complexes. This limitation is particularly problematic for studying anion binding and activation, where challenging electronic structures are common, as evidenced by Raghavachari's work on anion receptors and electrocatalytic Mo-complexes. A robust, automated method that retains G5's efficiency for routine molecules while correctly treating strongly correlated cases is a critical unmet need in computational chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a real-time multi-reference diagnostic (e.g., D1, T1, or %TAE) into the G5 computational sequence will allow for the automatic detection of problematic systems and the adaptive substitution of a multi-reference correlation treatment, yielding a single composite method with G5-level accuracy across both single-reference and multi-reference chemical spaces.
proposals.method: We will develop a software framework that executes the standard G5(MP2) sequence but computes a diagnostic (like the D1 diagnostic from CCSD calculations) after the initial CCSD(T) step. If the diagnostic exceeds a calibrated threshold, the workflow will branch: the high-level correlation energy component will be recalculated using a multi-reference method (e.g., CASPT2 or MRCI+Q with an automatically selected active space) on the same reference geometry and basis set. This adapted energy component will then be reintegrated into the standard G5 additive scheme for the final extrapolation to the complete basis set limit.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first broadly applicable, 'black-box' composite thermochemistry method that is both accurate and reliable for the full range of molecular systems, from organic closed-shell molecules to open-shell transition metal complexes. It will directly enable high-throughput computational screening and mechanistic study of challenging systems relevant to catalysis and anion recognition, areas of active interest in the Raghavachari group.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in the fusion of a robust multi-reference diagnostic directly into the deterministic workflow of a Gaussian-n theory, creating an adaptive method that intelligently changes its own computational identity based on the system's electronic structure, which is a significant advance over static composite schemes or manual intervention.

proposals.title: Adaptive Domain Decomposition for Composite Quantum Chemistry: A Fragment-Based AMR Strategy for Accurate Thermochemistry of Large Anion Receptors
proposals.motivation: High-accuracy composite quantum chemistry methods like G4 are computationally prohibitive for large molecules, such as the macrocyclic anion receptors studied in our group. This limits the ability to predict reliable binding energies and electronic properties for complex host-guest systems. A strategy to focus computational resources on chemically critical regions is urgently needed to extend high-accuracy methods to molecular systems of practical interest.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) philosophy from CFD, we hypothesize that a domain decomposition algorithm can automatically identify and treat chemically critical molecular fragments with a high-level composite method (e.g., G4), while embedding them in a lower-level quantum mechanical environment, achieving near-G4 accuracy at a dramatically reduced computational cost.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm to partition a large molecule into fragments and assign a 'chemical criticality' score based on local electronic descriptors (e.g., Wiberg bond orders, Fukui functions, or electrostatic potential variance). The top-scoring fragment(s) will be treated with the G4 composite method. The environment will be treated with DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D). The coupling will be achieved via an electronic embedding scheme within the ONIOM framework, where the high-level region's electrostatic potential is included in the environment's Hamiltonian, analogous to boundary condition handling in AMR. The algorithm will be validated on large anion-binding macrocycles and transition metal complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical software tool and protocol for applying composite-method accuracy to large molecules, directly enabling high-fidelity binding energy studies for complex anion receptors. It will establish a new paradigm for multi-scale quantum chemistry by formally importing and adapting proven concepts from large-scale numerical simulation in other fields.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit conceptual and algorithmic translation of Adaptive Mesh Refinement from continuum fluid dynamics to discrete quantum molecular systems, automating the identification of 'critical regions' for high-level treatment, which goes beyond traditional, manually defined QM/MM or ONIOM partitions.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Quantum Chemical Analysis of Anion Binding in Large Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Assemblies
proposals.motivation: High-accuracy composite quantum chemical methods like G4 are crucial for predicting reliable binding energies in anion-molecule complexes, as demonstrated in Raghavachari's recent computational landscape study. However, these methods are computationally prohibitive for the large, flexible macrocycles and supramolecular lattices (e.g., bambusurils) central to modern anion receptor and optical materials research. A scalable, accurate computational framework is needed to bridge this gap between methodological rigor and system size.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Fragment Molecular Orbital (FMO) method, combined with an embedding scheme for charged species, can accurately reproduce G4-level anion binding energies and electronic properties for large supramolecular systems at a fraction of the computational cost.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark a hybrid FMO/embedding protocol. Large host molecules (e.g., bambusuril macrocycles) will be partitioned into fragments using the FMO2 method at the DFT level. The anion and its immediate binding pocket will be treated as a high-level quantum mechanical (QM) region embedded within the electrostatic potential of the remaining fragments, which are treated at a lower DFT level. The binding energy will be computed using a G4-like composite scheme (e.g., G4(MP2)) applied selectively to the QM region, with corrections from the FMO framework. We will validate against full-system calculations on smaller complexes from our prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated computational protocol for performing G4-quality calculations on anion binding in molecular systems exceeding 200 atoms. It will provide unprecedented insight into the cooperative binding and excitonic coupling in large, multi-anion assemblies, directly informing the design of next-generation optical anion sensors and separation materials.
proposals.novelty: While FMO is established for proteins, its tailored integration with high-accuracy composite methods (G4) and explicit treatment of anionic embedding for supramolecular chemistry is novel. This directly addresses the unique challenge of accurately modeling diffuse anionic charges within large, polarizable macrocyclic cavities.

proposals.title: A Periodic Embedded Composite Energy (P-ECE) Scheme for High-Accuracy Anion Binding and Catalytic Site Energetics on Surfaces
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of binding energies for anions, adsorbates, and reaction intermediates on solid surfaces is critical for designing catalysts and separation materials, but remains a major challenge. Standard periodic DFT lacks the accuracy of molecular composite methods like G4, while applying these high-level methods directly to extended systems is computationally intractable. A rigorous embedding framework that isolates a surface-active site for composite-level treatment within its periodic environment is needed to bridge this accuracy gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The energetics of a localized chemical process on a solid surface can be computed with near-chemical-accuracy (<1 kcal/mol) by applying a molecular composite energy protocol to a well-defined, Wannier-localized fragment, embedded in a one-electron potential derived from a periodic DFT calculation of the full system.
proposals.method: We will develop the P-ECE scheme by first performing a periodic DFT (e.g., PBE-D3) calculation of the surface or solid. Maximally localized Wannier functions (MLWFs) will define the finite 'active' fragment (e.g., an adsorption site and its immediate coordination sphere). The environment's electrostatic and Pauli-repulsion effects will be represented by a frozen density potential via the Projection-based Embedding (PbE) method. A composite energy calculation (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS with core-valence and scalar relativistic corrections) will be performed on the embedded fragment. The workflow will be automated and tested on benchmark systems like anion binding to modified silica surfaces or CO2 reduction intermediates on MoS2.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first generally applicable computational scheme to achieve G4-level accuracy for localized processes in periodic materials. It will provide definitive benchmark data for anion-surface interactions and catalytic activation energies, directly impacting the rational design of anion receptors and electrocatalysts. The method establishes a new paradigm for multi-scale quantum chemistry in materials science.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of the rigorous, multi-step correction philosophy of molecular composite methods with periodic embedding theory, creating a targeted high-accuracy tool for solids, unlike previous QM/MM or cluster model approaches which lack systematic error control or proper periodicity.

proposals.title: A Coarse-Grained Embedding Strategy for High-Accuracy Composite Thermochemistry of Extended Solids
proposals.motivation: High-accuracy composite quantum chemistry methods like G4 are indispensable for predicting molecular thermochemistry and reaction barriers, but their prohibitive cost prevents application to extended solids and surfaces. This gap limits our ability to model key processes in catalysis and energy materials with chemical accuracy. The analogous challenge in polymer physics was overcome by coarse-graining, suggesting a viable path for electronic structure methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electronic environment of an infinite solid can be coarse-grained into an effective embedding potential, enabling the application of the high-accuracy G4 composite scheme to a finite, representative cluster to predict solid-state properties with near-molecular accuracy.
proposals.method: We will extract representative clusters from prototype ionic and covalent solids. A periodic DFT calculation (e.g., using SCAN-rVV10) will provide benchmark properties (e.g., band gap, cohesive energy). The cluster will be treated with the G4 method while embedded in a parameterized model potential representing the bulk. We will use a machine-learning optimizer (e.g., Bayesian optimization) to tune the potential's parameters (e.g., point charges, dielectric screening, pseudopotentials) to minimize the difference between the cluster-in-potential and benchmark periodic results for a training set.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel multi-scale framework that extends the accuracy of composite quantum chemistry methods to periodic systems. It will provide a validated protocol for calculating formation energies and defect properties in solids with an accuracy currently reserved for molecules, directly impacting the computational design of materials for anion binding and electrocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of coarse-graining concepts from polymer physics to high-level electronic structure theory and the specific use of the G4 scheme as the local high-fidelity solver, a combination not explored in pre-2026 quantum embedding or QM/MM methods for solids.

proposals.title: Inverse Optimization of Element-Specific Parameters for the G4 Composite Method Using Atomic Energy Residuals
proposals.motivation: Composite quantum chemistry methods like G4 are vital for accurate thermochemical predictions but rely on universal empirical corrections that can lead to non-systematic errors for diverse elements. Pre-2026, the community recognizes that error cancellation in these methods is imperfect, with residuals for atomic energies varying significantly across the periodic table. A data-driven, element-specific re-parameterization is needed to embed systematic error correction directly into the method's foundation, enhancing its reliability for molecular systems containing heteroatoms, including the anions and transition metal complexes central to Raghavachari's research.
proposals.hypothesis: The systematic residual errors in G4 atomic energies, derived from high-level reference data, can be used as constraints in an inverse optimization to solve for a new set of element-specific or group-specific underlying parameters. This intrinsic tuning will yield a systematically-corrected G4 method (G4-SC) with improved accuracy and transferability for molecular thermochemistry, particularly for systems involving anions and transition metals.
proposals.method: We will first compute the residual errors (G4 vs. benchmark data like ATcT) for neutral atoms across the periodic table. These residuals will form the target dataset for a large-scale inverse optimization using a Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The optimization will solve for adjusted, physically-grounded parameters within the G4 framework—such as basis set coefficients, HF exchange percentages, or MP2 scaling factors—that are allowed to vary by element or by groups (e.g., main-group, transition metals, halogens). The parameterization will be validated by computing formation enthalpies for a broad test set of molecules and complexes, including anion-binding macrocycles and catalytic intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available parameter set for the G4 method that intrinsically minimizes systematic atomic errors. It will provide a generalizable blueprint for data-driven refinement of composite methods. For the field of computational anion and molecular recognition, it will deliver a more reliable tool for predicting binding energies and electronic structures with enhanced accuracy.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively treats systematic atomic residuals not as post-hoc corrections but as direct constraints for an inverse parameter optimization, moving beyond universal scaling. It fuses systematic error analysis from benchmark studies with inverse problem-solving techniques not previously applied to the parameterization of multi-step composite methods like G4.

proposals.title: Systematic Assessment of a Unified DFT Approach for Accurate and Efficient Prediction of Anion Binding Thermodynamics in Macrocyclic Receptors
proposals.motivation: Computational studies of anion binding, a core theme in Raghavachari's group, often employ a hybrid methodology where geometries and zero-point energies (ZPE) are calculated at a lower-cost DFT level, while final single-point energies use a higher, more accurate level. This pragmatic approach introduces inconsistencies and potential error cancellation, making it difficult to assess the intrinsic accuracy of a given functional for the entire thermodynamic cycle. A rigorous, unified protocol is needed to establish reliable computational benchmarks for designing selective anion receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a single, consistently parameterized density functional theory (DFT) method with an appropriate basis set and dispersion correction for all components of the calculation—geometry optimization, vibrational frequencies (ZPE), and final electronic energy—will yield anion binding affinities that are both more internally consistent and of comparable or superior accuracy to hybrid protocols when benchmarked against reliable experimental data.
proposals.method: We will select a diverse set of anion-macrocycle complexes from Raghavachari's prior computational landscape study, covering various anions (e.g., halides, oxyanions) and receptor topologies. For each system, we will perform full geometry optimizations and frequency calculations (to obtain ZPE and thermal corrections) at the same theoretical level. The final Gibbs free energy of binding will be computed directly from these unified calculations. We will systematically test modern DFT functionals (e.g., ωB97X-D, B3LYP-D3, M06-2X) with consistent basis sets (e.g., def2-TZVP) and implicit solvation models (SMD), comparing results to both hybrid-method predictions and available experimental binding constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear, validated protocol for computing anion binding thermodynamics, reducing methodological ambiguity in the field. It will establish which unified DFT approaches are most reliable, offering a streamlined and more transparent workflow for the computational design of next-generation anion receptors and catalysts, such as those for CO2 reduction. The resulting benchmark data will be a valuable resource for the supramolecular and inorganic chemistry communities.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid methods are commonplace, a systematic investigation deliberately avoiding them to isolate the performance of a single, consistent level of theory for all thermodynamic components in anion binding is lacking. This study directly tests the sufficiency of modern DFT functionals as standalone tools for this critical application, moving beyond reliance on error cancellation between theory levels.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Implicit Solvation Models for Accurate Solution-Phase Geometries and Thermochemistry of Anion-Macrocycle Complexes
proposals.motivation: Accurate computational modeling of anion binding in solution is critical for designing selective receptors and understanding electrocatalytic mechanisms, as highlighted by your recent work. Current composite thermochemical methods (e.g., CBS-QB3, G4) often employ gas-phase geometries, neglecting solvent-induced structural changes that significantly impact binding free energies. This gap limits the predictive power of calculations for experimental solution-phase systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimizing molecular geometries using density functional theory (DFT) with advanced implicit solvation models (e.g., SMD, COSMO-RS) will yield structures more representative of the solution phase, leading to significantly improved accuracy in subsequent high-level composite method calculations of anion binding affinities and reaction energies.
proposals.method: We will select a benchmark set of anion-macrocycle complexes from your prior computational landscape study. Initial geometries will be optimized using the ωB97X-D functional with the SMD implicit solvation model for key solvents (water, acetonitrile). These solvated geometries will serve as input for single-point energy calculations using the G4(MP2) composite method. The protocol will be systematically compared to the traditional approach using gas-phase-optimized geometries. Binding free energies will be computed and validated against available experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical computational protocol for obtaining reliable solution-phase thermochemistry for anion binding and related reactions. It will directly enhance the predictive modeling of receptor design and catalytic cycles, such as those for CO2 reduction, by bridging the gap between high-level gas-phase energetics and solvated molecular structures.
proposals.novelty: While implicit solvation is standard for single-point energies, its systematic integration into the geometry optimization step specifically for high-level composite thermochemical methods applied to anion binding is underexplored. This study uniquely focuses on quantifying the structural and energetic consequences for macrocyclic and catalytic complexes central to your research program.

proposals.title: A Dual-Method Correction Scheme for G3//B3LYP: Systematic Error Reduction for Anion and Transition Metal Complexes
proposals.motivation: The G3//B3LYP composite method is a cornerstone for high-accuracy thermochemical calculations in large molecular systems, including anion receptors and catalytic complexes, due to its computational efficiency. However, its reliance on B3LYP geometries and frequencies introduces systematic errors, particularly for non-covalent interactions and transition metal species, limiting predictive reliability. A robust, generalizable correction scheme is needed to bring G3//B3LYP accuracy closer to the full G3 standard without its prohibitive cost.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the error between G3//B3LYP and full G3 energies is systematic and predictable within specific molecular classes (e.g., anion complexes, macrocycles, Mo-diimine catalysts). A small, strategically chosen set of parallel full G3 calculations can be used to derive class-specific linear correction parameters, significantly improving accuracy for all members of that class.
proposals.method: We will first compute benchmark full G3 and G3//B3LYP energies for a curated training set of ~50 molecules, focusing on anion-binding macrocycles, small-molecule anion complexes, and Mo-based catalytic intermediates from the researcher's prior work. Using multivariate regression, we will correlate the G3//B3LYP error with class-specific descriptors (e.g., anion charge, binding energy at B3LYP, metal coordination number). The derived correction functions will be validated on a separate test set, and the scheme will be implemented as an automated post-processing script for Gaussian output.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source correction protocol that dramatically increases the accuracy of G3//B3LYP for critical systems in supramolecular and catalytic chemistry. It will provide more reliable thermodynamic data for anion binding affinities and catalytic cycles, directly impacting the design of new receptors and catalysts. The methodology establishes a template for error-correcting other composite methods.
proposals.novelty: While error correction for density functionals is common, applying a targeted, class-specific machine-learning-inspired correction to a high-level composite method like G3//B3LYP is novel. The approach moves beyond generic benchmarks to create actionable, chemistry-specific tools, bridging the gap between high-accuracy and high-throughput computational workflows.

proposals.title: High-Precision Coupled-Cluster Calculations for Transition Metal Atomic Ionization Energies: A Benchmark Study of 3d and 4d Elements
proposals.motivation: Accurate ionization energies (IEs) of transition metal atoms are critical for benchmarking density functionals, calibrating spectroscopic data, and understanding catalytic processes, yet high-level theoretical benchmarks remain scarce. While coupled-cluster methods like CCSD(T) are the gold standard for molecular systems, their systematic application to open-shell transition metal atoms, with significant electron correlation and relativistic effects, is computationally challenging and underexplored. This creates a gap between molecular and atomic quantum chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully implemented coupled-cluster protocol, incorporating scalar relativistic effects and systematic basis set extrapolation, can predict first and second ionization energies for 3d and 4d transition metals with chemical accuracy (<1 kcal/mol) relative to experimental values, providing definitive benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will employ the high-level coupled-cluster singles, doubles, and perturbative triples (CCSD(T)) method as implemented in the CFOUR and MRCC packages. Calculations will start with all-electron correlation-consistent basis sets (cc-pwCVnZ-DK, n=T,Q,5) for 3d elements and relativistic effective core potentials (RECPs) for 4d elements. Scalar relativistic effects will be included via the Douglas-Kroll-Hess (DKH) or exact two-component (X2C) Hamiltonians. We will perform rigorous basis set extrapolation to the complete basis set (CBS) limit and assess the impact of higher-order excitations (CCSDT(Q)) for select cases. The workflow will be automated for systematic study across the series.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a definitive, publicly available dataset of coupled-cluster quality ionization energies for transition metal atoms, serving as a critical benchmark for developing and validating new density functionals and lower-cost quantum chemical methods. It will also elucidate the relative importance of correlation, relativity, and basis set effects in these fundamental atomic properties, bridging high-accuracy molecular and atomic quantum chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While CCSD(T) is standard for molecules, its systematic, CBS-limit application to generate a comprehensive benchmark set for transition metal atomic ionization energies is novel. The project directly transfers the rigor of molecular anion-binding and catalytic reaction studies to the foundational atomic domain, a non-obvious extension given the focus on complex molecular systems in the researcher's recent work.

proposals.title: Systematic High-Accuracy Calculation of Transition Metal Electron Affinities via Quantum Chemical Composite Methods
proposals.motivation: Accurate electron affinities (EAs) for transition metals are crucial for understanding redox chemistry, catalysis, and materials science, yet experimental data is scarce and often inconsistent. High-level computational benchmarks are limited, creating a knowledge gap that hinders the predictive modeling of electron transfer processes in complex systems like the anion-binding receptors and electrocatalytic complexes studied in our group.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the quantum chemical composite (QCI) methodology, previously validated for anion binding energies of molecular complexes, can be systematically scaled and adapted to provide benchmark-quality electron affinities for first-row (3d) and second-row (4d) transition metal atoms with chemical accuracy (< 1 kcal/mol).
proposals.method: We will employ the focal-point analysis approach, systematically converging toward the complete basis set (CBS) limit using coupled-cluster theory with singles, doubles, and perturbative triples [CCSD(T)]. Calculations will utilize the MRCC and CFOUR programs. The protocol will involve: 1) generating reference geometries and energies with high-level methods (e.g., CCSD(T)/cc-pwCVQZ), 2) correcting for core-valence correlation using specialized basis sets, 3) incorporating scalar relativistic effects via the Douglas-Kroll-Hess Hamiltonian, and 4) performing rigorous uncertainty analysis. The workflow will be automated for the entire 3d and 4d series.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, systematically derived set of benchmark EAs for transition metals, resolving long-standing experimental discrepancies. The resulting dataset will serve as a critical reference for calibrating lower-cost DFT functionals and for informing studies in anion coordination chemistry, electrocatalysis (e.g., CO2 reduction), and the design of redox-active materials.
proposals.novelty: While composite methods are used for main-group EAs, their systematic application to the challenging electronic structure of transition metals, with their high density of states and strong correlation effects, is novel. This project directly translates our group's expertise in high-accuracy anion energetics from molecular receptors to fundamental atomic properties.

ideas.title: Mapping the Domain of Utility for Perturbative and Quadratic CI Methods: A Predictive Benchmark for Correlation Treatment Selection
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a simple sufficiency claim to create a predictive map for electronic structure method selection. We will execute a large-scale, stratified benchmark covering organic, inorganic, and transition metal systems, focusing on challenging properties like reaction barriers, non-covalent interactions, and spectroscopic constants. The performance of MP4 and QCISD will be quantitatively compared to both higher-level coupled-cluster benchmarks and experimental data where available. The final product is a data-driven protocol or software tool that recommends the minimum computationally feasible method to achieve a user-specified accuracy for a given chemical problem, clearly delineating the boundaries of MP4/QCI reliability.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Hierarchical Composite Methods with Post-CCSD(T) Correlation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a composite quantum chemistry method that fundamentally inverts the traditional workflow. Instead of applying a fixed hierarchy of basis sets to a single, pre-defined high-level correlation method like CCSD(T), the method employs a decision framework to adaptively select and combine multiple advanced correlation techniques—such as CCSDT, CCSDT(Q), or selected CI methods—with optimally chosen basis sets for each component. The selection is guided by heuristics or low-cost diagnostics that identify which electron correlation effects (e.g., triple excitations, non-dynamical correlation) and which regions of basis set space are most critical for a given molecular system. This results in a composite model that is both more efficient and systematically improvable beyond the CCSD(T) ceiling, as it is not bound to a single correlation approximation or a linear basis set sequence.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Quantum Chemistry: A Domain Decomposition Approach from Computational Fluid Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts the domain decomposition strategy of Adaptive Mesh Refinement from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the problem of scaling high-accuracy quantum chemistry methods like G4 to large molecules. In CFD, AMR efficiently solves large-scale simulations by concentrating computational effort on complex sub-domains (like shock fronts) while using cheaper methods elsewhere. The adapted research idea involves developing an algorithm to automatically identify 'chemically critical' fragments within a large molecule (e.g., reaction centers or regions with unusual electronic structure). The expensive G4 (or similar composite) method is then applied only to these fragments, while the molecular environment is treated with a computationally cheaper quantum mechanical method. A key challenge is the adaptation of the embedding or coupling scheme to seamlessly integrate the different levels of theory, much like boundary conditions couple different mesh resolutions in AMR, thereby achieving high accuracy at a fraction of the full-system cost.

ideas.title: Active Learning for G4 Error Prediction to Enable Accurate Large-System Applications
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning for error prediction directly into the workflow of applying G4 theory to larger molecular systems. Instead of using a static ML model trained on a small test set, the framework employs an active learning strategy where tentative G4 calculations on new, complex molecules identify regions of high predictive uncertainty. These strategically chosen, larger systems then become targets for full, careful G4 analysis (or higher-level reference data where possible) to generate new, highly informative error data points. This cycle continuously expands and refines the ML error model's domain of applicability, systematically reducing the need for prohibitively expensive full G4 calculations on every large molecule while maintaining and even improving accuracy as the system size increases.

ideas.title: Adapting the G4 Composite Scheme for Solids via Coarse-Graining from Polymer Physics
ideas.description: The challenge of scaling the high-accuracy but computationally intensive G4 composite quantum chemistry scheme to extended solids or surfaces is analogous to the problem of simulating long polymer chains in polymer physics. The analogous field solved this by developing coarse-graining techniques, where groups of atoms are mapped to effective interaction sites, with parameters derived from high-fidelity simulations of smaller systems. This solution is adapted by proposing a multi-scale electronic structure method: a high-level G4 calculation is performed on a carefully chosen, finite 'representative cluster' extracted from the solid. This cluster is then embedded in a periodic effective potential that coarse-grains the rest of the infinite lattice. The parameters of this embedding potential are determined by requiring that properties (e.g., band gap at a high-symmetry point, cohesive energy) of the cluster-in-potential model reproduce those from a benchmark, periodic calculation (e.g., using a high-quality DFT functional or a small-cell coupled-cluster reference) for a training set of materials. This adapts the core idea of preserving accuracy for key properties while managing computational cost through strategic simplification of the extended environment.

ideas.title: Adaptive Computational Synthesis via Dynamic Pathway Planning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a fixed, predefined composite recipe of calculations is optimal for all molecules. By drawing an analogy from synthetic organic chemistry's retrosynthetic analysis, we propose a system that adapts the computational recipe per molecule. In the analogous field, chemists do not use a single reaction sequence for all targets; they dynamically plan a synthesis pathway by deconstructing the complex target into simpler units. Adapting this, the research idea is to create a computational framework that analyzes a target molecule and, based on its specific structural and electronic characteristics, dynamically plans and executes a tailored sequence of computational methods (e.g., DFT, coupled-cluster, neural network potentials). This adaptive pathway planner would select methods from a available toolkit to build up the desired property prediction, optimizing the trade-off between accuracy and resource expenditure for each unique molecular input.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Derivation of Systematically-Corrected, Element-Specific G4 Parameters
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the inverse problem approach with systematic error analysis to develop a novel parameterization strategy for composite quantum chemistry methods like G4. Instead of applying a universal empirical correction, the systematic residual errors for neutral atoms across the periodic table—identified from high-level reference data—become the target dataset for a large-scale inverse optimization. The core innovation is to treat these residuals not as mere deviations to be patched, but as direct constraints to solve for a new set of underlying, physically-grounded parameters (e.g., basis set parameters, empirical scaling factors) that are element-specific or grouped by electronic structure. This yields a method where the parameters are intrinsically tuned to reproduce accurate atomic energies, thereby embedding systematic error cancellation into the method's foundation and promising improved transferability to molecular systems.

ideas.title: Geometry-Optimized Density Functional for Composite Accuracy (GO-DFCA)
ideas.description: This research develops a novel density functional theory (DFT) framework where a specialized, parameterized functional is not just evaluated at a final geometry, but is actively involved in and shaped by the geometry optimization process itself. The functional's parameters are iteratively adjusted during the optimization to minimize the difference between its own energy/gradient and a target high-level composite method's (e.g., G4) energy/gradient at that same nuclear configuration. This results in a single, self-consistent functional that inherently yields geometries, vibrational frequencies, and reaction paths with near-G4 accuracy directly from a DFT calculation, eliminating the need for separate, costly composite method optimizations or single-point corrections on DFT geometries.

ideas.title: Adapt composite energy schemes for excited states via multi-fidelity structural engineering methods
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the multi-fidelity modeling approach used in structural engineering for seismic analysis to the problem of calculating energies for electronically excited states. In the analogous field, predicting a building's complex 'excited' state during an earthquake is managed not by a single monolithic simulation, but by a hierarchy of models of varying cost and accuracy, where cheaper methods guide the targeted application of expensive ones. The adapted research idea applies this principle by developing a composite energy calculation scheme for molecular excited states. It would use low-cost quantum chemical methods to map the excited-state landscape and identify key diagnostic features (e.g., dominant configurations, charge transfer character, orbital relaxation effects). This information would then strategically direct where to apply computationally expensive, high-accuracy wavefunction-based calculations, creating a hierarchical, context-aware composite procedure that balances accuracy and cost for excited-state energy predictions.

ideas.title: Periodic Embedded Composite Energy (P-ECE) Scheme for Solids and Surfaces
ideas.description: This method adapts the high-accuracy, multi-level philosophy of molecular composite schemes like G4 for extended systems by combining them with periodic embedding theory. It first performs a periodic DFT calculation to obtain the solid's or surface's full electronic structure and uses this to define a localized, finite 'active' fragment via Wannier localization or projected atomic orbitals. A composite energy calculation (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS corrected for core-valence and relativistic effects) is then executed on this embedded fragment, with its wavefunction constrained by a one-electron potential representing the periodic environment. This approach directly targets the energetics of localized processes like defect formation or adsorption, providing G4-level accuracy for the region of interest while maintaining computational tractability through rigorous embedding.

ideas.title: Adaptive Gaussian-5 Theory with On-the-Fly Multi-Reference Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of a next-generation Gaussian-5 (G5) thermochemical protocol with intelligent multi-reference diagnostics to create an adaptive, high-accuracy computational chemistry method. The core innovation is a G5 theory framework that incorporates an on-the-fly diagnostic, such as a T1 or D1 metric, to automatically detect problematic multi-reference character during its standard computational sequence. For systems where strong correlation is identified, the workflow seamlessly switches key components—like the underlying reference wavefunction or correlation treatment—to a more appropriate multi-reference method, such as CASPT2 or MRCI, before completing the final G5 energy extrapolation. This results in a single, robust, and black-box composite method that delivers G4/G5-level accuracy for routine organic molecules while automatically providing correct, chemically accurate results for traditionally challenging systems like diradicals, transition states, and open-shell transition metal complexes without user intervention.

ideas.title: Diagnostic-Driven, Multi-Protocol Composite Approach for Transition State Energetics
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the electronic structure diversity of transition states by integrating an initial diagnostic screening step. Based on automated multi-reference character assessment, the method dynamically selects either a single-reference composite protocol (optimized for transition state geometries) or a specialized multi-reference composite protocol. Both pathways incorporate basis set extrapolations and correlation treatments specifically parameterized for the flat potential energy surfaces and partial bonds characteristic of transition states. The final output provides not only accurate barrier heights but also quantifies uncertainty based on diagnostic outcomes and protocol convergence.

ideas.title: Adaptive Surrogate-Model-Driven Optimization for High-Level Potential Energy Surfaces
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the prohibitive cost of direct high-level optimization by using an efficient, adaptive workflow. A fast, lower-level electronic structure method conducts the primary geometry optimization. Selectively, at strategically chosen points along the path, single-point calculations at the target high level of theory are performed. These high-fidelity data points train a local surrogate model (e.g., a Gaussian process or neural network) that corrects the lower-level gradient and energy. The optimization then proceeds on this corrected, hybrid surface, converging to a geometry that effectively reflects the high-level theory at a fraction of the computational cost.

ideas.title: Calibrating Atomic Energy Predictions via a Metrological Reference Network
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from metrology and geodesy to the problem of systematic residual errors in calculated atomic energies. In geodesy, a global network of tide gauges provides reference points to calibrate and remove biases from satellite altimetry data. Analogously, the project will establish a calibrated network of reference atoms—selected across the periodic table—whose total energies are known with benchmark, near-exact accuracy from primary quantum methods. By analyzing the deviations of standard computational methods (e.g., density functional theory or coupled-cluster) from these reference values, a systematic error function dependent on atomic properties (like Z, number of valence electrons, shell structure) will be derived. This function will then be used to apply post-hoc corrections, thereby systematically reducing residual errors in predicted total energies for any neutral atom, effectively creating a traceable calibration standard for computational atomic physics.

ideas.title: Develop a Gaussian-5 theory via a composite, multi-fidelity approach
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes developing Gaussian-5 (G5) theory by drawing an analogy from composite modeling in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). In CFD, the high cost of Direct Numerical Simulation is managed by using its data to correct and train faster, lower-fidelity models, creating a practical hybrid framework. Adapted to quantum chemistry, G5 would be architected as a composite, multi-fidelity theory. Instead of a single method, it would be a framework that uses ultra-high-accuracy reference calculations (e.g., CCSDT(Q) on large basis sets) to systematically derive correction factors and training parameters for a hierarchy of more affordable components, such as those in G4 theory (e.g., basis set extrapolation, empirical higher-level corrections). The core innovation is an intelligent, system-dependent protocol that allocates computational effort where it most improves accuracy, strategically blending different levels of theory to achieve superior accuracy for thermochemical properties at a manageable computational cost, succeeding the more rigid, single-pathway G4 approach.

ideas.title: A Single-Determinant, Post-CCSD(T) Surrogate via Machine-Learned, System-Specific Orbital Transformations
ideas.description: This research direction aims to develop a machine-learning framework that identifies a non-empirical, system-specific unitary transformation of the Kohn-Sham orbitals. The transformed orbitals define a single Slater determinant from which standard DFT functionals can predict energies and properties with accuracy surpassing CCSD(T) and rivaling more advanced wavefunction methods, but at essentially DFT cost. The key is that the transformation is learned to implicitly encode strong correlation and post-CCSD(T) effects into a mean-field-like picture, making the advanced correlation 'visible' to a simple functional. This creates a new paradigm where the challenge to CCSD(T) comes not from a more complex wavefunction ansatz, but from a smart, learned pre-processing step that collapses the complexity into a single-determinant model.

ideas.title: Adapting Composite Methods for Transition States via High-Precision Surveying Techniques
ideas.description: The original idea proposes applying composite methods (e.g., Gaussian-1) to calculate accurate energies for transition states, a challenge because these methods are designed for stable species. Drawing an analogy from high-precision geodetic surveying, where a network of stable reference points is used to triangulate the position of an inaccessible feature, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea involves using a composite procedure to calculate highly accurate energies for stable species along a reaction coordinate (reactants, products, intermediates). These become calibrated reference energies. The transition state's energy is then determined relative to this reference network via a lower-level calculation that is carefully corrected or interpolated using the established baseline, thereby extending the accuracy of composite methods to reaction barriers without requiring their direct, unstable application to the saddle point itself.

ideas.title: Stochastic Geometry Relaxation for Quantum Monte Carlo
ideas.description: This research direction integrates stochastic quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) techniques for electronic energy extrapolation with a stochastic geometry optimization method, moving beyond fixed B3LYP reference structures. The core idea is to perform geometry relaxations using forces computed from QMC energies (e.g., from Diffusion Monte Carlo or auxiliary-field QMC) or their systematically improvable surrogates, all within a Monte Carlo sampling framework for nuclear coordinates. This creates a unified, ab initio stochastic pipeline that avoids the bias of a single, density-functional-theory-determined geometry. The final output is a molecular structure and total energy with quantified uncertainties, both rigorously converging toward the exact non-relativistic solution without reliance on semi-empirical functional choices.

ideas.title: A System-Specific, First-Principles Correction Model via Analogy to Climate Downscaling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fixed higher-level correction scheme by replacing empirical atom-based corrections with a physically derived, system-specific model, drawing an analogy from climate science. In climate modeling, the analogous problem of coarse global predictions is solved by 'downscaling,' where local physics and geography are incorporated to refine forecasts for a specific region. Adapting this solution, we propose treating the base quantum chemical calculation as a coarse global model and developing a downscaling procedure that uses a system-specific, low-cost physical or descriptor-based model to compute first-principles corrections tailored to the unique electronic structure and geometry of each target system, thereby moving beyond one-size-fits-all empirical parameters.

ideas.title: Replace additive atomic corrections with a many-body embedding potential
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that empirical corrections for atomic energies (e.g., in density functional theory) must be additive and element-specific. By drawing an analogy from Dynamical Mean-Field Theory in condensed matter physics—which solves strongly correlated lattice problems via a self-consistent quantum impurity embedding—we propose a first-principles, non-additive approach. Each atom is treated as an impurity embedded in an effective potential that represents its specific chemical environment (coordination, bond types, oxidation state). The required energy correction is computed by solving a localized, high-level electronic structure problem for this embedded impurity, making the correction inherently environment-dependent, non-additive, and derived from a more fundamental theoretical framework rather than empirical fitting.

ideas.title: Explicitly Correct for Basis Set Superposition Error via a Counterpoise-Inspired, System-Specific Protocol
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that basis set superposition error (BSSE) becomes negligible with large basis sets by advocating for its explicit, system-specific accounting. The analogy comes from experimental psychology, where the 'practice effect'—a systematic bias in repeated testing—is not assumed away but is instead quantified using a control group and subtracted from the experimental results. Adapting this solution, the proposed research would develop a standardized protocol for quantum chemistry calculations: for a given molecular system, a BSSE calibration factor is first determined by applying a counterpoise-like method to a carefully chosen representative fragment or model interaction. This system-specific correction, rather than a generic assumption, is then applied to the energy calculation of the full system. This ensures BSSE is actively corrected based on direct measurement, improving accuracy and reliability across different system types and basis sets.

ideas.title: State-Specific Adaptive Composite Scheme for Electronically Excited States
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive composite procedure for excited states that overcomes the one-size-fits-all flaw. It integrates a diagnostic front-end that characterizes the physical nature of the target excited state (e.g., valence, charge-transfer). The core composite scheme then dynamically configures its hierarchy of methods, basis sets, and corrections based on this diagnosis, using parameters optimized for each state category. This ensures robust accuracy across diverse excited-state chemistries, from simple valence excitations to challenging multi-configurational or charge-separated states.

ideas.title: Meta-Optimized Molecular Recipe Generation via Learned Cost-Accuracy Policy
ideas.description: This system replaces arbitrary adaptation with a principled, learned decision process. A meta-learner, trained via reinforcement learning on a broad chemical space, proposes calculation recipes by dynamically selecting and sequencing computational methods (e.g., DFT functionals, basis sets, solvation models). Each proposed recipe is evaluated in-silico for its estimated cost and predicted accuracy, with the latter informed by uncertainty quantification. The policy is optimized to construct recipes that reliably converge to a user-specified accuracy target with minimal computational expense, ensuring both robustness and efficiency for novel molecules.

ideas.title: Relativistic Multi-Reference Diagnostics for G4 Theory
ideas.description: This research direction develops a new, adaptive extension to the G4 composite method. It integrates a multi-reference diagnostic tool that identifies systems with significant static correlation or open-shell character. For systems flagged as problematic for single-reference methods, the framework automatically triggers a switch to a CASSCF-based treatment for the core electronic structure, upon which the relativistic corrections (e.g., via Douglas-Kroll-Hess or exact two-component methods) are then consistently applied. This synergy allows for the first-principles, automated application of high-accuracy relativistic quantum chemistry specifically to the challenging systems that need it most, moving beyond the limitations of applying relativistic corrections only to single-reference, closed-shell paradigms.

ideas.title: System-Specific Correlation Models to Replace Empirical Higher-Level Corrections
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the use of fixed, atom-typed higher-level corrections (HLC) in composite methods by proposing a system-specific, physically derived model to replace them. It directly tests the hypothesis from Idea B—that MP4 and quadratic configuration interaction (QCI) capture all essential correlation—by using these methods as a physically justified baseline to decompose the correlation energy for any given system. The synergy emerges by constructing a model that predicts, from lower-level descriptors, where and why more expensive correlation treatments (e.g., CCSD(T)) are genuinely required, moving beyond empirical corrections to a first-principles, system-adaptive correlation scheme. This aims to provide both a rigorous validation of the sufficiency of certain methods and a predictive tool for automating method selection in multiscale modeling.

ideas.title: Integrated On-the-Fly Geometry Optimization in Composite Methods
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that equilibrium geometries must be known or computed separately before applying a high-level composite method. Drawing an analogy from real-time computer graphics, where complex object deformations are generated procedurally during simulation rather than pre-computed, we propose integrating geometry optimization directly into the composite method's workflow. The analogous solution uses fast, simplified models (like reduced-order physics) to generate plausible outcomes in real-time. Adapted to computational chemistry, this means embedding a fast, low-cost geometry predictor (such as a machine-learned potential or semi-empirical method) within the iterative cycle of a composite electronic structure method (e.g., CCSD(T)/CBS). This embedded model would propose geometry updates 'on-the-fly' based on the ongoing high-level energy evaluations, effectively performing the optimization concurrently with the final energy calculation. The fast model's parameters could be periodically refined or corrected by the high-level method itself, ensuring convergence to the correct high-level geometry without ever performing a separate, standalone optimization at a lower level of theory.

ideas.title: Diagnostic-Triggered Density-Corrected DFT for Robust Geometry and ZPE Refinement
ideas.description: This improved protocol introduces an automated diagnostic step to assess the likelihood of significant density-driven errors in the initial B3LYP calculation. Systems that pass the diagnostic proceed unchanged, preserving efficiency. For systems flagged by the diagnostic, the workflow automatically triggers a targeted density correction: the geometry is re-optimized using a robust, dispersion-corrected meta-GGA or hybrid functional (e.g., ωB97M-V) known for accurate densities, and the zero-point energy is recalculated consistently on this improved geometry. This approach ensures the computational cost of DC-DFT is incurred only when necessary and that the correction is applied in a physically consistent manner.

ideas.title: Variance-Controlled Stochastic Extrapolation to Quantum Chemical Limits via Embedded Control Variates
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach tackles the fundamental noise problem in stochastic extrapolation by designing a unified framework where variance reduction is intrinsic to the limit estimation. The system first generates a correlated set of stochastic calculations at progressively higher accuracy tiers, using techniques like reptation Monte Carlo or auxiliary-field QMC with improved trial wavefunctions. Crucially, it employs physically motivated, embedded control variates—such as leveraging low-level deterministic coupled-cluster energies—to absorb the bulk of the variable signal, leaving a low-variance residual for stochastic processing. This enables the construction of a precise, statistically well-behaved sequence whose convergence trend can be reliably extrapolated using robust estimators, yielding a numerically stable and uncertainty-quantified prediction for the exact Hartree-Fock or correlation energy.

ideas.title: Incorporate anharmonic ZPE corrections via a perturbative mapping from a reference potential
ideas.description: This idea aims to improve the accuracy of zero-point vibrational energy (ZPE) calculations by moving beyond the harmonic approximation, which fails for soft modes and dissociative states. Drawing an analogy from classical fluid dynamics, where complex turbulent flows are modeled by correcting a simpler potential flow solution, we propose to treat the harmonic oscillator ZPE as a computationally efficient baseline. The adapted solution is to develop a perturbative mapping framework where anharmonic corrections are derived from a limited set of reference high-level calculations on a representative training set or along key degrees of freedom. These corrections are then formulated as transferable functions or scaling factors applicable to the harmonic reference potential, enabling efficient incorporation of anharmonic ZPE effects for larger molecules and reactions without the prohibitive cost of full anharmonic analysis for every structure.

ideas.title: Inverse Calibration via Empirical Bayesian Inference from Experimental Energy Database
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the inverse problem in seismic tomography—where Earth's internal structure is inferred from seismic wave data—to the domain of computational chemistry. The analogous problem is deriving optimal G4 quantum chemistry correction parameters from a large database of experimental energies. Just as geophysicists use a Bayesian framework and massive datasets to solve for subsurface properties, here we propose an Empirical Bayesian inference method. The experimental energy database serves as the global set of observations. The method statistically inverts this data to solve for the underlying G4 parameters, treating it as a high-dimensional optimization that incorporates chemical knowledge as priors, thereby directly learning the correction scheme from experimental evidence rather than calibrating it on small training sets.

ideas.title: Consensus-Validated Surrogate Modeling with Disagreement-Aware Uncertainty
ideas.description: This approach constructs training and benchmark datasets using a consensus of high-level computational methods, rather than a single composite method like G4. Regions of chemical space where the reference methods disagree are explicitly identified and treated as high-uncertainty zones. Machine learning or reduced-physics models are then trained against this consensus data, incorporating a loss function that penalizes deviation from the consensus while quantifying epistemic uncertainty arising from reference disagreement. This yields faster surrogate models that are robust, aware of their own limitations, and avoid inheriting the systematic biases of any single reference method.

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Reference Diagnostics via Anomaly Detection and Ensemble Switching
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Network Security/Intrusion Detection Systems, where a hybrid system uses a fast primary screen and switches to a deeper, ensemble-based analysis upon detecting anomalies. The analogous problem in computational chemistry is the efficient identification and accurate treatment of systems with strong electron correlation, where single-reference methods fail. The adapted research idea is to integrate a multi-reference diagnostic as an 'anomaly detector' within a standard quantum chemistry workflow. For systems passing the diagnostic, standard DFT or CCSD proceeds. For systems flagged as 'problematic,' the workflow automatically switches to and executes a pre-defined ensemble of CAS-based methods (e.g., CASSCF, CASPT2, DMRG) to ensure a robust, multi-reference treatment, mimicking how an IDS escalates analysis for suspicious network traffic.
